proposals.title: Adversarial AI for Stress-Testing Scholarly Integrity: Generating Synthetic Research Misconduct to Improve Detection Tools
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of AI tools to screen journal submissions for plagiarism, data fabrication, and image manipulation creates an urgent need to evaluate their robustness. As of 2025, detection systems are typically validated on known or simplistic cases of misconduct, leaving them potentially vulnerable to novel, AI-generated forms of deception. This research addresses a critical gap in ensuring the reliability of AI-assisted scholarly publishing by proactively stress-testing these safeguards.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using adversarial AI methods to generate sophisticated, synthetic cases of research misconduct will reveal significant, exploitable weaknesses in current AI-powered detection tools, and that these vulnerabilities can be systematically addressed to create more robust detection systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative adversarial network (GAN) framework where a generator model, trained on legitimate scientific text and image datasets, learns to produce fabricated data, manipulated images, and plagiarized text with the objective of deceiving a suite of detector models (e.g., GPT-based classifiers, image forensic tools). The detectors will be state-of-the-art systems used or proposed for screening submissions. The adversarial training will employ reinforcement learning and gradient-based attacks (like Projected Gradient Descent) to iteratively refine the generator's outputs, creating a benchmark dataset of 'hard' adversarial examples of misconduct.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel benchmark dataset of AI-generated adversarial misconduct cases for tool evaluation, 2) a formal methodology for proactively assessing the limits of AI-based integrity screens, and 3) specific, actionable guidance for developers and journals on hardening detection systems against emerging AI-facilitated threats to research integrity.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on using AI to detect misconduct, this proposal inverts the problem by using AI to generate it for defensive purposes. This adversarial, red-teaming approach to scholarly integrity tooling is novel and moves beyond post-hoc analysis to proactive stress-testing.

proposals.title: Ethical and Practical Frameworks for AI-Autonomous Scholarly Publishing: A Simulation-Based Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scientific publishing, from manuscript preparation to peer review screening, has prompted calls for stringent human oversight to prevent misconduct and ensure quality. However, this requirement creates a significant bottleneck, potentially stifling the pace of dissemination. This research addresses the critical, pre-2026 tension between the need for efficiency and the ethical imperative of accountability in scholarly communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for well-defined, formulaic scientific sub-genres (e.g., systematic review protocols, data descriptors), AI-driven publishing workflows with minimal human verification can be designed to meet or exceed current ethical and quality standards, as measured by simulated error and misconduct rates.
proposals.method: The study will employ a multi-phase, simulation-based approach. First, we will define a taxonomy of 'low-risk' manuscript types based on pre-2025 publication data. Second, we will develop and train a prototype AI agent using a transformer-based architecture (e.g., a fine-tuned GPT-3/4 or BERT variant) on curated, high-quality corpora of these document types. Third, we will create a simulated publishing environment to test the agent's performance, injecting known ethical breaches (e.g., plagiarism, data fabrication) and quality flaws. Key metrics will include detection/avoidance rates, computational provenance logging, and comparative analysis against human-only workflows.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirically grounded, ethical framework for tiered human oversight in AI-assisted publishing. It will offer journal editors and publishers concrete guidance on when and how to relax full human verification, alongside a validated risk-assessment model. The project will directly inform new AI-specific publishing guidelines called for in recent literature.
proposals.novelty: While existing work insists on human-in-the-loop oversight, this proposal uniquely investigates the conditions under which such oversight can be responsibly minimized, moving beyond precaution to proactive, evidence-based policy design for autonomous scientific communication.

proposals.title: Developing a Framework for Universal Publisher-Wide Standards on the Use of Artificial Intelligence in Scholarly Publishing
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of AI tools into scholarly publishing—for tasks from manuscript screening to language editing—has created a fragmented and ethically fraught landscape. As of 2025, individual publishers and journals are developing ad-hoc policies, leading to inconsistency, confusion for authors and reviewers, and significant risks regarding accountability, transparency, and research integrity. This lack of coordination undermines trust in the publication process and the scholarly record.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a consensus-based, cross-publisher consortium can develop a unified, principled framework of standards for AI use in publishing that is both ethically robust and practically implementable, thereby increasing transparency and trust.
proposals.method: The project will employ a modified Delphi method to build consensus among a consortium of major publishers, editors, and ethics experts. We will first conduct a systematic review and content analysis of existing (pre-2026) AI policies from leading journals. Using this analysis, we will draft a preliminary framework of standards, which will then be iteratively refined through structured, anonymized survey rounds with the consortium panel. The final framework will be validated through a pilot implementation study with a subset of participating publishers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first concrete, collaboratively developed blueprint for universal AI use standards in academic publishing. It will directly address the 'guidance gap' identified in prior literature by moving from abstract ethical principles to actionable, cross-publisher policy. The resulting framework will serve as a critical resource for standard-setting bodies like COPE and ICMJE.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has called for guidance, this project is novel in its specific focus on creating a *universal*, *publisher-driven* standard through formal consensus methodology, moving beyond single-journal recommendations to address the systemic nature of the problem.

proposals.title: An Ethical and Technical Framework for AI-Generated Scholarship Detection in Academic Publishing
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly writing creates a dual problem: it can enhance productivity but also threatens research integrity through undisclosed use and potential misconduct. As noted in recent calls for guidance, journals currently lack reliable, standardized tools to screen submissions for AI-generated text, creating an urgent need for detection methods to uphold transparency and trust in the scientific record. This project addresses the ethical imperative, highlighted by Resnik's prior work, to develop oversight mechanisms for new research tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid detection model, combining fine-tuned transformer-based classifiers with interpretable stylistic and statistical features, can reliably distinguish AI-generated scholarly text from human-authored work across diverse academic disciplines. Furthermore, we posit that such a tool must be developed within an explicit ethical framework to guide its responsible deployment.
proposals.method: We will construct a labeled dataset of human-authored papers (from arXiv, PubMed) and AI-generated counterparts using GPT-4 and other contemporary (pre-2026) models prompted to mimic academic writing. The core technical approach will fine-tune a RoBERTa or DeBERTa model for binary classification. This will be augmented with a feature engineering pipeline capturing known AI text characteristics (e.g., burstiness, syntactic uniformity, specific token probabilities). Model performance will be evaluated on held-out datasets and against adversarial examples (e.g., lightly paraphrased AI text).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, open-source detection tool prototype for journal editors and a validated benchmark dataset for future research. It will also deliver a parallel ethical analysis and proposed governance guidelines, directly extending Resnik's scholarship on AI ethics, to inform policies on the tool's appropriate use, limitations, and integration into submission screening processes.
proposals.novelty: While general AI text detectors exist, this project is novel in its dedicated focus on the specialized domain of scholarly communication and its explicit integration of technical development with ethical analysis from the outset, creating a dual-output framework tailored to the needs of academic publishing.

proposals.title: From Informed Consent to Interactive Disclosure: A Tiered Framework for AI Transparency in Scientific Research
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scientific research and publishing, from manuscript screening to data analysis, has created an urgent need for robust transparency practices. Current approaches, such as simple disclosures in journal submissions, risk being perfunctory and fail to ensure genuine understanding or accountability, analogous to the limitations of a basic patient brochure in medicine. This gap threatens research integrity and public trust, necessitating a more sophisticated ethical framework grounded in established bioethical principles.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the layered, interactive model of informed consent from medical ethics to AI transparency will produce a more effective framework that improves user comprehension, facilitates meaningful choice, and enhances accountability compared to static, one-off disclosure methods.
proposals.method: The research will first conduct a conceptual analysis mapping the core elements of informed consent (disclosure, comprehension, voluntariness) onto AI use contexts in science. It will then develop a prototype tiered disclosure interface, where a core plain-language explanation is the default, with clickable layers providing access to technical details (e.g., model cards), audit trails, and accountability contacts. The framework's efficacy will be tested via controlled user studies with researchers and journal editors, employing pre- and post-exposure questionnaires to measure comprehension gains and using established usability metrics (e.g., System Usability Scale) to evaluate the interface.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide concrete, ethically-grounded guidance for journals, institutions, and funding bodies on implementing meaningful AI transparency. It will contribute a novel, testable framework to the fields of research ethics and human-computer interaction, moving the discourse beyond simple disclosure mandates toward verifiable understanding and accountability.
proposals.novelty: While the need for AI transparency is recognized, this proposal uniquely applies the mature, interactive model of medical informed consent as a direct analog, arguing for a dynamic, comprehension-verified process rather than a static disclosure event, which is a non-obvious shift in the current policy debate.

proposals.title: Embedded, Dynamic Governance: A Technical Framework for Real-Time AI Risk Validation in Scientific Research Workflows
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scientific research, from data analysis to manuscript drafting, has outpaced the development of effective governance. Current ethical guidelines, as noted in Resnik's recent work, are often static and decoupled from the research process, creating a compliance gap. This project addresses the urgent need to make ethical adherence an operational, real-time component of research, preventing misconduct and bias at the point of tool use rather than through retrospective review.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating real-time, context-aware validation modules directly into common research software environments will significantly improve researcher compliance with evolving AI ethics standards and reduce the incidence of methodological and ethical risks compared to reliance on standalone guidelines.
proposals.method: We will develop lightweight, open-source plug-in modules for Jupyter Notebooks and electronic lab notebook platforms using their existing extension APIs (e.g., Jupyter Widgets). These modules will integrate rule-based validation engines, informed by a structured ontology of AI risks (e.g., data provenance, algorithmic bias, plagiarism). A central, wiki-style platform will allow the community to document new use cases and risks; these contributions will be curated and converted into updated validation rules using natural language processing techniques (specifically, fine-tuned BERT models for classification and rule extraction) to dynamically update the local plugins.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a functional, open-source technical framework for embedded AI governance, moving the field from abstract principles to executable code. It will also generate a novel, community-curated dataset mapping specific AI research tools to identified risks and mitigations, providing empirical grounding for future policy and tool development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on publishing guidelines, this project operationalizes them through seamless, real-time integration into research workflows. The closed-loop system, where community-reported edge cases automatically refine validation protocols, represents a shift from static, top-down governance to a dynamic, participatory model.

proposals.title: A Framework for Machine-Actionable Provenance in Synthetic Data: Ethical and Technical Foundations for Bias Control
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of generative AI to create synthetic datasets for training scientific models introduces profound ethical and reliability challenges. Pre-2026, there is a recognized gap in governance: while journals and funding bodies call for disclosure of AI use, current practices offer passive documentation that does not enable active control over downstream bias and error. This creates a crisis of provenance, where researchers cannot systematically trace model failures back to specific flaws in the synthetic data pipeline, undermining scientific integrity and replicability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a structured, machine-actionable provenance standard for synthetic data will enable the development of auditing tools that can actively map data-generation artifacts to model performance errors, thereby creating a functional control mechanism for bias mitigation that current ad-hoc disclosures lack.
proposals.method: The project will first develop a metadata schema extending pre-2026 standards like PROV-O to encode key attributes: generative model (e.g., GPT-4, Stable Diffusion version), prompt history, known bias audits, and intended use constraints. We will then build a prototype auditing tool that ingests this provenance, using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) and influence function analysis to quantify the correlation between specific data-source features and model error patterns across fairness metrics. A case study will test the framework on a biomedical NLP task using a synthetic clinical notes dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, implementable standard for synthetic data provenance that moves beyond narrative disclosure to enable active bias control. It will contribute directly to the emerging ethics of AI in research by offering journals and institutions a technical mechanism to screen for and remediate synthetic data-related misconduct, as called for in recent guidance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on documenting AI use or detecting bias in models, this approach uniquely fuses ethical disclosure requirements with active algorithmic auditing by making provenance itself a machine-readable control layer, creating a closed-loop system for bias mitigation specific to synthetic data origins.

proposals.title: A Framework for Ethical Disclosure and Labeling of AI-Generated Synthetic Data in Scientific Publishing
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of generative AI models (e.g., GANs, diffusion models) for creating synthetic data in research introduces significant ethical and epistemic risks. Without clear standards, the use of such data threatens research integrity by obscuring the provenance of findings, complicating reproducibility, and potentially enabling new forms of data fabrication or undisclosed augmentation. This creates an urgent need for guidance, as highlighted by recent calls for AI ethics in science, to ensure transparency and trust in scholarly work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a consensus-based, multi-stakeholder framework for synthetic data disclosure can be developed and will be perceived by researchers, journal editors, and ethicists as both feasible and necessary for maintaining research integrity in the age of generative AI.
proposals.method: The project will employ a mixed-methods approach. First, a systematic review of pre-2026 literature and existing journal policies will catalog current practices and gaps. Second, we will conduct a Delphi study with a panel of 30-40 experts (including data scientists, journal editors, research ethicists, and funding agency representatives) to iteratively develop and refine a set of disclosure standards. Third, we will prototype a metadata schema (e.g., an extension of the DataCite or Schema.org standards) to encode key information such as the generative model used, seed data provenance, and known statistical limitations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a concrete, actionable set of guidelines and a technical metadata specification for labeling synthetic data in publications and data repositories. It will directly inform journal submission policies and data sharing mandates, providing a critical tool for upholding transparency. The project will advance the scholarly discourse on AI ethics in research by moving from abstract principles to implementable standards.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has discussed AI ethics broadly or focused on AI for text generation, this project specifically targets the under-addressed problem of synthetic *data* provenance and labeling in empirical research. It uniquely combines ethical analysis with practical standards development and technical specification, bridging the gap between policy and implementation.

proposals.title: A Metrological Framework for Uncertainty Quantification in AI-Generated Text Detection: Toward Standardized and Auditable Evidence
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of generative AI in scholarly and journalistic writing has created an urgent need for reliable detection tools to combat plagiarism and misinformation. However, existing detection algorithms, such as GPTZero or OpenAI's classifier (as of 2025), produce opaque scores prone to false positives and lack standardized measures of reliability. This undermines their ethical use in high-stakes contexts like journal submissions or legal evidence, where decisions based on flawed detection can cause significant harm.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying metrological principles of systematic error analysis and uncertainty quantification to AI-text detection algorithms will yield a detection report with a defined confidence interval and uncertainty budget, providing a more scientifically robust and ethically justifiable foundation for decision-making than a simple binary classification or probability score.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement (GUM) framework. For a chosen detection model (e.g., a fine-tuned RoBERTa or DeBERTa architecture), we will identify and model major uncertainty sources: Type A (statistical, via Monte Carlo simulations on varied text samples) and Type B (systematic, via analysis of training data bias, model calibration error, and cross-architecture consistency). An uncertainty budget will be constructed, and detection outputs will be reformulated as a measured value with an expanded uncertainty interval (e.g., k=2 for ~95% confidence).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete methodological template for quantifying and reporting the reliability of AI-detection tools, moving the field beyond accuracy metrics alone. It will produce a standardized detection report format that is auditable and transparent, directly addressing calls for new ethical guidance on AI in research and publishing by scholars like Resnik.
proposals.novelty: While uncertainty quantification is standard in metrology and some machine learning subfields, its systematic application to the specific task of AI-generated text detection to produce an auditable 'measurement' report is novel. This fusion creates a new evaluative paradigm focused on error sources and confidence, not just classification performance.

proposals.title: The Homogenization Hypothesis: A Controlled Experiment on the Impact of Personalized Large Language Models on Conceptual Divergence in Scholarly Ideation
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI, particularly personalized LLMs, into scholarly workflows presents a novel ethical and epistemic challenge. Pre-2026 guidance focuses on misconduct and transparency, but a deeper risk is the potential for these tools to inadvertently constrain conceptual diversity by reinforcing dominant paradigms and citation patterns. This study addresses a critical gap in understanding how AI assistance shapes the very generation and diffusion of novel ideas, which is fundamental to scientific progress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that participants using personalized LLM support for literature review and hypothesis generation will produce outputs with significantly lower computational and perceived novelty, and that these ideas will demonstrate less robust diffusion in simulated peer networks, compared to those using non-personalized LLMs or no LLM support.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3x2 factorial experiment with early-career researchers (n=180). Conditions vary by LLM support type (personalized GPT-4 fine-tuned on user's prior writing, generic GPT-4, none) and task framing. Participants will complete a standardized ideation task. Output originality will be measured via: 1) BERT-based embedding similarity and TF-IDF novelty scores against a corpus of relevant literature; 2) blind expert ratings using a validated novelty scale. Subsequently, a diffusion simulation will model idea spread using an agent-based model where participant outputs serve as seeds, tracking adoption metrics across network conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical evidence quantifying the causal effect of personalized LLMs on scholarly divergence. It will yield concrete metrics for 'conceptual homogenization risk' and generate actionable, evidence-based guidance for researchers, journal editors, and institutions on mitigating AI-induced epistemic convergence.
proposals.novelty: While existing research examines AI's role in writing or misconduct, this study uniquely isolates the impact of *personalization* on ideation *and* traces its consequences through a simulated diffusion network, combining experimental psychology with computational social science methods.

proposals.title: Beyond Human Oversight: A Framework for Identifying and Mitigating Unsupervisable AI-Generated Content in Scholarly Publishing
proposals.motivation: Current ethical guidelines for AI in research, including those from journals and funding bodies, universally mandate human oversight of AI-generated content. However, this principle assumes human authors can always effectively supervise AI outputs. This assumption is untested and may be false in critical scenarios involving high-volume, high-complexity, or epistemically opaque AI systems, creating latent risks for research integrity and public trust.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exist definable and predictable scenarios in scholarly work where, due to cognitive, temporal, or epistemic constraints, human authors cannot provide meaningful oversight of AI-generated content, rendering the standard 'human-in-the-loop' safeguard insufficient.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods approach. First, a conceptual analysis will define 'effective supervision' and its necessary conditions. Second, we will conduct structured scenario-building workshops with researchers and editors to identify candidate failure modes. Third, we will analyze these scenarios using a framework adapted from safety engineering (e.g., 'Normal Accident Theory') and epistemology to categorize the root causes of oversight failure (e.g., volume, velocity, complexity, opacity). Finally, we will develop a diagnostic checklist for journals and authors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic critique of the sufficiency of human oversight as a normative standard for AI in research. It will produce a concrete, actionable framework to help journals, institutions, and researchers anticipate and mitigate supervision failures, moving ethical guidance from principle-based to risk-based. It will directly inform Resnik's call for new guidance on AI tools.
proposals.novelty: While existing literature prescribes human oversight, no work pre-2026 systematically challenges its feasibility or maps its failure conditions within scholarly practice. This proposal shifts the debate from *whether* oversight is needed to *when* and *how* it can realistically be achieved.

proposals.title: From Farm to Fork to Lab to Reader: An Empirical Test of a Provenance-Based Disclosure Framework for AI-Assisted Scholarly Work
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into research and publishing, from literature review to manuscript drafting, creates a crisis of trust for readers. Current disclosure practices are ad hoc and binary, failing to convey the nuanced role of AI in the scholarly process. This gap mirrors historical challenges in complex food supply chains, where opaque processes eroded consumer confidence.
proposals.hypothesis: Readers' perceptions of a scholarly work's credibility, trustworthiness, and value will be significantly and positively influenced by access to a structured, verifiable provenance label detailing the type, extent, and purpose of AI assistance, compared to a simple binary disclosure or no disclosure.
proposals.method: We will first develop a structured disclosure schema (an 'AI Provenance Label') by adapting frameworks like the EU's food traceability regulations (EC 178/2002) to scholarly workflows, specifying fields for tool type, function, and human oversight level. Using a controlled, factorial survey experiment, we will recruit academic readers and present them with research abstracts accompanied by different disclosure formats. We will measure dependent variables (credibility, trust, perceived value) using validated scales (e.g., McCroskey's source credibility measures) and analyze results using ANOVA and regression models to isolate the effect of provenance detail.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirically tested, standardized framework for AI disclosure in scholarship, moving beyond ethical principles to practical guidance. It will generate concrete data on how transparency mechanisms affect reader trust, directly informing journal policies (e.g., for *Accountability in Research*, *Science and Engineering Ethics*) and funding agency mandates.
proposals.novelty: The proposal is novel in its analogical application of food traceability solutions to a scholarly communication problem and its empirical, reader-centric focus on trust perception, which contrasts with the dominant pre-2026 discourse focused on author ethics and misconduct detection.

proposals.title: Mapping Linguistic Artifacts to Reader Trust: An Empirical Study of AI-Assisted Scholarly Text
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly writing necessitates new ethical guidance, as highlighted by Resnik's recent work. Current policies focus on binary disclosure, but pre-2026 forensic stylometry (e.g., GLTR, stylometric classifiers) shows AI leaves detectable linguistic signals. It is unknown how these signals, independent of disclosure, influence reader trust, a core pillar of scholarly communication.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, forensically identifiable linguistic features in scholarly text (e.g., low lexical diversity, predictable word choice, syntactic uniformity) causally influence readers' perceptions of credibility and trustworthiness, independent of explicit AI use disclosure.
proposals.method: We will generate experimental text variants using GPT-4 (pre-2026 SOTA) with controlled prompting to produce specific forensic markers (quantified via GLTR and BERT-based stylometric tools). These texts will be embedded in a controlled reader-response study with academic participants, using validated scales to measure perceived credibility, authority, and trust. A multivariate regression model will map the intensity of specific linguistic features to trust scores.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first empirical, causal model linking detectable AI stylistic artifacts to reader judgment. It will move ethical guidance beyond simple disclosure toward evidence-based standards for acceptable AI use that minimize unintended trust erosion. The predictive model can inform journal submission screening tools and author training.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies AI text or studies disclosure effects, this fuses forensic detection with perceptual psychology to causally link specific, machine-identified linguistic artifacts to human trust, creating a granular map for policy.

proposals.title: Perceived Credibility and Trust in AI-Assisted Scholarly Publications: A Reader-Centric Empirical Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current ethical guidance for AI in research (Resnik, 2024) focuses on author responsibilities, such as disclosure and preventing misconduct. However, the ultimate impact of AI-assisted work depends on reader reception and trust, a critical but understudied dimension. As journals and publishers grapple with disclosure policies, understanding how readers perceive and trust scholarship known to be AI-assisted is essential for developing effective communication norms and maintaining the integrity of the scholarly record.
proposals.hypothesis: Readers will perceive AI-assisted scholarly abstracts as less credible and trustworthy than human-written abstracts, even when the AI-assisted text is of comparable or superior quality, and this effect will be moderated by the explicitness and framing of the AI disclosure statement.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-wave, randomized online survey experiment using a representative sample of academic readers. Participants will be presented with scholarly abstracts in various conditions (human-written, AI-assisted with no disclosure, AI-assisted with explicit disclosure, AI-assisted with framed disclosure). We will measure perceived credibility, trustworthiness, and author expertise using validated psychometric scales (e.g., McCroskey's Source Credibility Scale). Data analysis will employ factorial ANOVA and multiple regression models to test main and interaction effects, controlling for factors like reader discipline and familiarity with AI tools.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical, reader-centered data to inform ethical and practical guidelines for AI disclosure in scholarly publishing. It will offer concrete evidence for journals and publishers on how different disclosure statements affect reader trust. The findings will extend the ethical discourse on AI in research beyond author conduct to include the communicative relationship between scholarship and its audience.
proposals.novelty: While existing work examines author-side ethics and detection tools, this study inverts the focus to the reader, a pivotal yet neglected stakeholder. It empirically tests how ethical norms like transparency manifest in reader perception, moving the debate from abstract principle to measurable consequence.

proposals.title: Ethical Scrutiny of the Fine-Tuning Assumption: Copyright, Consent, and Authorship in Generative AI for Science
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scientific research and publishing, for tasks like literature review and manuscript drafting, often proceeds on the implicit assumption that using published scholarly work for model training is ethically and legally unproblematic. This assumption is increasingly contested, as pre-2026 legal cases and policy debates highlight tensions between copyright law, fair use doctrines, and the consent of authors whose work is used. This creates urgent guidance gaps for researchers, publishers, and developers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic analysis of copyright frameworks, publisher policies, and authorial expectations will reveal significant, unresolved ethical and legal vulnerabilities in the common practice of using scholarly corpora for AI fine-tuning, challenging the presumption that it is a low-risk activity.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods approach. First, a legal-ethical analysis will map existing copyright law (e.g., U.S. fair use factors) and emerging case law (pre-2026) onto common fine-tuning practices. Second, a content analysis of major journal publisher policies (e.g., Elsevier, Springer Nature, PLOS) and AI ethics statements will be conducted. Third, a survey and focus groups will be designed to gauge researcher and author perceptions of consent and attribution regarding the use of their publications in AI training datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide concrete, evidence-based guidance for the scientific community, moving beyond abstract principles to actionable recommendations for ethical fine-tuning practices. It will contribute a structured framework for evaluating the permissibility of using specific corpora, directly informing the development of institutional policies and AI ethics checklists for researchers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has addressed AI ethics broadly or AI in publishing, this proposal uniquely focuses on the specific, under-scrutinized 'fine-tuning' phase, linking technical practice directly to concrete copyright analysis and the novel ethical concept of 'scholarly consent' within research ecosystems.

proposals.title: The Erosion of Authorial Voice: An Empirical and Computational Model of Stylistic Degradation from AI-Assisted Writing
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly writing tools threatens the integrity of authorial voice, a cornerstone of academic attribution and trust. Current ethical guidance and detection tools focus on AI-generated text, but lack a principled, evidence-based framework for assessing the subtle degradation of human style caused by AI assistance. This gap leaves journals, institutions, and researchers without clear standards for evaluating the authenticity of AI-assisted work.
proposals.hypothesis: Different levels of AI writing assistance produce a quantifiable and predictable 'degradation signature'—a multi-dimensional erosion of stylistic coherence and individual authorial markers—that can be modeled and inversely detected.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, controlled study where human participants produce texts across multiple sessions with varying levels of AI assistance (e.g., from editing suggestions to full drafting). We will quantify stylistic dimensions (lexical diversity, syntactic complexity, consistency of stylistic 'fingerprints' via stylometric tools like JStylo) to map the degradation signature. This empirical data will train a novel inverse detection model, likely a transformer-based architecture (e.g., BERT or RoBERTa) fine-tuned not to classify AI text, but to identify the statistical absence of consistent human stylistic features indicative of erosion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical model linking AI assistance levels to measurable stylistic outcomes, offering concrete data for ethical guidelines on 'appropriate use.' It will also pioneer a new forensic detection paradigm focused on the loss of human style rather than the presence of AI artifacts, directly addressing the limitations of current classifiers.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its synergistic, two-pronged design: it first empirically defines the phenomenon of stylistic degradation, then uses that definition to build a detection model, creating a closed-loop system for validation and refinement that moves beyond binary AI/human classification.

proposals.title: Engineering for Originality: An Empirical and Ethical Framework to Counteract Homogenization in LLM-Assisted Scholarly Writing
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) like GPT-4 into academic workflows, including for drafting and literature synthesis, presents a profound ethical and epistemic risk: the systemic homogenization of scholarly discourse. Pre-2026 ethical guidance, including Resnik's own work, focuses on transparency and misconduct but lacks empirical grounding and proactive mechanisms to safeguard the foundational academic value of originality. This project addresses the critical gap between recognizing a potential threat and designing technically-embedded, evidence-based solutions to mitigate it.
proposals.hypothesis: The use of personalized, fine-tuned LLMs (e.g., AutoGen agents) for scholarly writing, without specific countermeasures, will produce measurable homogenization in argument structure, phrasing, and conceptual framing across authors within a sub-field. Conversely, integrating 'pro-originality' constraints into the LLM's fine-tuning and prompting pipeline can significantly reduce this homogenization effect.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled experiments using the AutoGen framework to simulate scholarly writing tasks. A cohort of LLM agents will be fine-tuned on identical corpora from a target sub-field (e.g., bioethics) and asked to generate literature reviews. We will quantify homogenization using NLP metrics (e.g., BERT-based semantic similarity, n-gram overlap, syntactic diversity). Subsequently, we will iteratively engineer and test technical interventions: 1) a 'novelty-check' module using outlier detection on embedding spaces, 2) mandatory prompt diversification algorithms, and 3) fine-tuning with adversarial datasets that reward stylistic deviation. The empirical results will directly inform the drafting of a dynamic ethical policy framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirically-validated, technical-ethical framework for pro-originality LLM use in academia. It will contribute a novel methodology for quantifying LLM-induced homogenization and a set of testable technical constraints for tool developers. For research ethics, it moves beyond post-hoc guidelines to offer enforceable, process-embedded standards for preserving intellectual diversity.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of controlled experimentation on multi-agent LLM systems with proactive ethical guideline creation is novel. Unlike existing AI ethics work that proposes principles abstractly, this project designs and validates specific technical levers (e.g., novelty-check modules) as core components of the ethical framework itself.

proposals.title: Ethical Frameworks for Personalized Academic Agents: A Delphi Study on the Responsible Use of Fine-Tuned LLMs in Scholarly Writing
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of generative AI tools like AutoGen for academic writing creates novel ethical challenges. Current guidelines for AI in research are generic and fail to address the specific risks of personalized, fine-tuned LLMs, which can amplify individual researcher biases, obscure intellectual contribution, and create new forms of plagiarism or data fabrication. Without targeted policy, the integrity of scholarly publishing is at risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured consensus-building process with key stakeholders will identify a core set of actionable, domain-specific ethical principles for the use of personalized LLMs in academic writing that are not adequately covered by existing broad AI ethics guidelines.
proposals.method: This study will employ a modified Delphi method. First, a systematic literature review of pre-2026 AI ethics guidelines and publishing policies will establish a baseline. Second, a panel of ethicists, journal editors, senior researchers, and research integrity officers will iteratively rate and discuss draft principles over three survey rounds, using statistical analysis of convergence (Kendall's W) to achieve consensus. The final framework will be structured around core ethical concepts from Resnik's prior work: authorship, transparency, accountability, and integrity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dedicated, consensus-based ethical and policy framework for the use of personalized LLMs in academic writing. It will provide concrete guidance for researchers, institutions, and journals, directly addressing the 'new guidance needed' gap identified in recent literature. The output will be a publishable set of principles and a template for institutional policy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work calls for general AI guidance, this proposal specifically targets the under-explored domain of *personalized* LLM agents in scholarship, moving from broad principles to implementable norms for a rapidly emerging practice. It applies a rigorous consensus methodology (Delphi) typically used in clinical ethics to a new problem in research ethics.

proposals.title: Designing for Accountability: A Technical Framework for Provenance and Attribution in Human-LLM Scholarly Co-Authoring
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly writing creates urgent ethical and practical challenges for authorship attribution, plagiarism detection, and research integrity. Current debates on liability and misconduct are abstract, lacking the technical evidence base needed for fair assessment. This gap hinders the development of clear ethical guidelines and trustworthy publishing practices for AI-assisted research.
proposals.hypothesis: A system architecture that instrumentally logs the human-LLM interaction process and probabilistically attributes output to training data influences can generate a forensically auditable transcript, thereby creating a concrete, technical foundation for accountability that precedes and informs legal and ethical interpretation.
proposals.method: We will design and prototype a modular co-authoring system that logs all user prompts, model parameters (e.g., temperature, top-p), and model outputs. The core technical innovation will integrate algorithms for training data attribution (e.g., based on gradient-based influence functions or k-nearest neighbor retrieval in embedding space for open-source models) to probabilistically link generated text segments to influential source data. This data will be compiled into a standardized, timestamped, and cryptographically signed interaction transcript and provenance report.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete technical architecture to move the debate from abstract liability to operational accountability, offering publishers and institutions a prototype system for evidence-based misconduct screening. It contributes directly to the development of new technical standards and ethical guidelines for AI use in research, a key gap identified in prior scholarship.
proposals.novelty: While existing work calls for guidance, this project proactively designs a functional system to generate the necessary evidence, integrating real-time interaction logging with nascent training data attribution methods specifically for the scholarly co-authoring context.

proposals.title: Distributing Accountability for AI-Generated Text: A Pharmaceutical Liability Analogy
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly work and publishing creates significant ethical and legal gaps regarding accountability for errors, misinformation, or plagiarism in AI-generated text. Current frameworks are inadequate, leaving developers, service providers, and users in a state of uncertain liability that stifles responsible innovation and use. This ambiguity mirrors historical challenges in product liability, necessitating a structured, principled solution.
proposals.hypothesis: A legal framework adapted from pharmaceutical product liability, which apportions responsibility among manufacturer, physician, and pharmacist, can be coherently mapped to the AI text generation ecosystem to establish a clear, fair, and practical chain of accountability among LLM developers, service providers, and end-users.
proposals.method: The research will employ a conceptual-analogical methodology. First, it will deconstruct the established three-tier pharmaceutical liability framework (manufacturer, learned intermediary, dispenser). Second, it will conduct a functional analysis of the AI text generation pipeline, identifying analogous roles (foundational model developer, service provider implementing personalization/fine-tuning, end-user). Third, using principles from Resnik's prior work on AI ethics guidance, it will analyze and adapt the liability criteria (e.g., duty to warn, control over risk, foreseeable misuse) to propose a tailored accountability model for AI-generated content.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide concrete, actionable guidance for policymakers, journal editors, and research institutions grappling with AI misconduct. It will advance the field of AI ethics by moving from abstract principle to a structured liability proposal, directly addressing the 'guidance needed' gap identified in recent literature. The framework will clarify obligations for each actor, promoting more responsible development and use of generative AI tools in research.
proposals.novelty: While AI liability is discussed, the systematic application of the mature, multi-actor pharmaceutical liability analogy to the specific problem of AI-generated text is novel. This approach moves beyond simplistic 'developer vs. user' binaries, offering a nuanced distribution of responsibility that reflects the complex, layered reality of modern AI systems.

proposals.title: A Provenance Framework for AI-Augmented Manuscripts: Applying Supply Chain Data Lineage to Research Integrity
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly work creates urgent ethical and practical challenges for research integrity, reproducibility, and attribution. Current disclosure practices are coarse and unverifiable, failing to provide the granular detail needed for proper oversight, as highlighted by recent calls for new guidance. This gap undermines trust in the scientific record and complicates editorial and peer-review processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting supply chain provenance models—specifically, the use of unique identifiers and immutable ledgers to track component origin and transformation—can create a transparent, machine-readable record of AI contributions to a manuscript, thereby enhancing accountability and reproducibility.
proposals.method: We will model manuscript creation as an intellectual supply chain. Each discrete AI intervention (e.g., LLM text generation, code execution, data analysis) will be tagged with a metadata token (using a schema like PROV-O) recording model version, prompt, timestamp, and input hash. These tokens will be linked into a provenance graph and stored in a lightweight, immutable structure such as a Merkle Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), implemented via a prototype tool integrated into common authoring workflows. The technical approach will leverage established cryptographic hashing (SHA-256) and distributed ledger concepts, but will be designed for local or institutional use to avoid scalability barriers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete technical framework and prototype for detailed AI contribution tracking, moving beyond narrative statements. It will offer scholarly publishers and institutions a practical model for verifying AI use, thereby contributing directly to the development of actionable ethics guidance and tools for AI in research.
proposals.novelty: While data provenance is a known concept in computer science, its application to the specific problem of AI contributions in scholarly manuscripts via an explicit supply chain management analogy is novel. This approach reframes the ethical transparency challenge as a logistical tracking problem, offering a structured, auditable solution not yet proposed in research ethics literature.

proposals.title: A Provenance-Based Framework for Tracking and Disclosing Generative AI Contributions in Scholarly Manuscripts
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scientific writing creates urgent ethical and transparency challenges for research integrity. Current disclosure practices are ad hoc and fail to capture the specific nature, extent, and influence of AI contributions, complicating authorship attribution, responsibility, and reproducibility. This gap undermines trust in scholarly publishing and highlights the need for new guidance, as identified in Resnik's prior work on AI ethics in research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting formal data provenance models from computational science can create a structured, auditable record of AI interactions during manuscript development, which will improve the accuracy, completeness, and utility of AI contribution disclosures compared to current narrative statements.
proposals.method: We will adapt the W3C PROV data model and ontology to define entities (e.g., text segments, prompts), activities (e.g., generation, paraphrasing, translation), and agents (human, AI system) involved in manuscript creation. A prototype tracking tool will be developed, using a combination of deterministic logging (for direct AI API calls) and heuristic analysis (for text similarity between drafts and AI outputs using algorithms like BERTScore) to infer provenance graphs. The framework will be tested via a case study analyzing the drafting process of sample research manuscripts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete technical and ontological framework to operationalize ethical guidance for AI use in research, moving beyond vague recommendations. It will offer publishers and journals a potential standard for mandatory, structured AI contribution disclosure, directly addressing the screening and misconduct challenges Resnik has previously outlined.
proposals.novelty: While data provenance is established in data science, its application to track the *creative process* of scholarly writing, specifically for AI-human collaboration, is novel. This approach moves disclosure from a subjective description to an objective, machine-readable audit trail.

proposals.title: A Proactive Adversarial Framework for Robust AI Authorship Attribution in Scholarly Publishing
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scientific writing, as noted in Resnik's recent work, creates an urgent need for reliable AI authorship attribution to maintain research integrity and trust. Current forensic methods are reactive, lagging behind the evolution of AI models and adversarial prompting techniques designed to evade detection. This creates a critical vulnerability in the peer-review and publishing ecosystem, where journals lack robust tools to screen for undisclosed AI-generated text.
proposals.hypothesis: A closed-loop, adversarial training framework that dynamically pits AI authorship detectors against a suite of probe language models engineered to simulate evolving AI writing styles will produce attribution tools that are significantly more generalized and robust than those developed using static datasets.
proposals.method: We will design a game-theoretic system with two agents: a detector (e.g., a fine-tuned transformer like RoBERTa or DeBERTa) and a generator (a suite of probe LLMs, including GPT-4, Claude, and open-source models). The generator will use reinforcement learning (e.g., Proximal Policy Optimization) with a reward function that encourages text to both mimic target human styles and evade the current detector. The detector will be continuously retrained on the adversarial outputs. This loop will create a self-evolving benchmark dataset, forcing the detector to learn generalized stylistic features rather than model-specific artifacts.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, proactive methodology for developing AI authorship detectors, moving the field from reactive forensics to anticipatory defense. It will yield a publicly available, dynamically updated benchmark for evaluating attribution tools, along with concrete ethical guidance for journals on the capabilities and limitations of such adversarial systems in screening submissions.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing work that tests detectors on static corpora, this approach actively engineers the evasion techniques it must defend against, creating a moving target that mirrors the real-world AI authorship arms race. It formalizes the attribution problem as a continuous adversarial game, a paradigm not yet applied in this domain.

proposals.title: Attributional Forensics for AI Training Data: A Stylometric Framework for Ethical Auditing and Provenance Verification
proposals.motivation: The ethical and legal use of training data is a critical, unresolved issue in AI development, as highlighted by ongoing lawsuits and policy debates about copyright and consent. Current practices rely on self-reported data cards, which are often incomplete and unverifiable. This creates a significant accountability gap, where claims of ethically sourced data cannot be technically falsified, undermining responsible research practices.
proposals.hypothesis: Stylometric and forensic linguistic techniques, adapted for large-scale corpus analysis, can reliably detect and attribute specific human-authored source texts within an AI model's training data, even in the absence of explicit labels or documentation.
proposals.method: The research will adapt and scale established stylometric methods (e.g., n-gram profiles, function word analysis, syntactic features) using high-performance computing. We will develop a pipeline that first creates stylistic fingerprints for known authors and publications from curated corpora. We will then apply unsupervised clustering and anomaly detection algorithms (e.g., DBSCAN, isolation forests) to a model's known or suspected training data to identify stylistic clusters and match them against the fingerprint database, testing attribution accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a concrete, evidence-based technical framework for auditing AI training data provenance, moving ethics discussions from abstract principles to falsifiable claims. It will provide a tool for journals, institutions, and regulators to assess compliance with data-use policies, directly addressing the call for new guidance on AI in research.
proposals.novelty: It shifts the focus of AI forensics from generated outputs back to the training corpus itself for attribution, and it applies small-scale stylometry to the novel, large-scale problem of data provenance auditing for ethical and legal accountability.

proposals.title: A Multi-Agent Adversarial Framework for the Continuous Auditing of AI in Scientific Research: A Proposal for Dynamic Oversight
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scientific research, from literature review to data analysis, creates urgent oversight challenges. Traditional peer review is static and slow, ill-suited to the dynamic, high-volume outputs of AI-assisted science. This gap necessitates new, automated tools for continuous scrutiny to prevent misconduct, ensure reproducibility, and manage novel risks like emergent biases or data contamination.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-agent system employing specialized adversarial AI auditors, operating continuously in a secure sandbox, can more effectively and comprehensively identify a wider range of potential failures (e.g., bias, hallucination, data leakage) in a target research AI than static, human-led audits, thereby providing a scalable model for dynamic oversight.
proposals.method: We propose a system architecture with specialized agents (e.g., for bias detection, logical consistency, data provenance) using established pre-2026 techniques like gradient-based adversarial attacks (e.g., FGSM), genetic algorithms for test case generation, and anomaly detection models. These agents will perpetually interact with a sandboxed instance of a target AI (e.g., a large language model fine-tuned for literature synthesis). A meta-reviewer agent, using a transformer-based model for multi-document summarization, will synthesize adversarial findings into structured risk reports. A human committee will periodically review and update the auditing agents' objectives and techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete technical framework for implementing continuous AI oversight, moving ethical guidance from principle to practice. It will contribute a novel benchmark for dynamic AI auditability and produce a prototype system that demonstrates how automated adversarial auditing can be integrated into the scientific workflow to enhance research integrity.
proposals.novelty: The approach shifts AI oversight from a periodic, human-centric activity to a continuous, automated, and multi-faceted adversarial process. It uniquely combines established adversarial machine learning techniques within a structured multi-agent system explicitly designed for the normative goals of scientific auditing, governed by a human-in-the-loop meta-review mechanism.

proposals.title: A Zero-Knowledge Proof Framework for Verifying AI Writing Tools: An Ethical and Technical Proposal for Automated Scholarly Audit
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly writing and publishing creates urgent ethical challenges, including verifying originality, ensuring compliance with safety norms, and preventing misconduct without compromising proprietary model data. Current audit methods are either superficial or require excessive transparency, creating a trust deficit. This research addresses the core tension in AI ethics between accountability and intellectual property, a key concern in recent guidance documents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cryptographic zero-knowledge proof protocols can be adapted to create a technically sound and ethically robust framework, enabling an AI writing tool to prove its outputs meet specific scholarly standards without revealing its sensitive internal data.
proposals.method: We will design a formal protocol where the AI model (prover) generates a succinct non-interactive zero-knowledge proof (zk-SNARK) attesting that its text generation process adhered to a predefined, verifiable circuit. This circuit will encode rules for plagiarism detection (via hashed reference checks), safety filter compliance, and attribution logic. The proof, verifiable by a third party (e.g., a journal's automated system), will confirm rule adherence while revealing nothing about the model's weights or training data beyond the claim's validity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel technical mechanism to operationalize ethical AI guidance in publishing, moving from principles to enforceable, privacy-preserving audits. It will contribute a formal model for 'trustless verification' in scholarly AI, offering publishers and researchers a tool to mitigate misconduct risks while respecting commercial and academic confidentiality.
proposals.novelty: While zero-knowledge proofs are established in cryptography, their application to audit the *internal compliance* of a generative AI model's textual output, rather than a simple financial transaction, is a novel interdisciplinary synthesis. It directly translates the 'blind trust' principle of peer review into a cryptographic protocol for AI oversight.

proposals.title: A Double-Blind Audit Framework: Adapting Scholarly Peer Review for the Validation of Generative AI Writing Tools
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scientific writing necessitates robust oversight mechanisms to ensure integrity and reliability, yet current approaches are ad-hoc and lack formal structure. Drawing on established principles from research ethics and scientific publishing, this project addresses a critical gap by proposing a systematic, ethical framework for auditing AI tools, directly responding to calls for new guidance on AI in research.
proposals.hypothesis: Formalizing and adapting the double-blind peer review process into a structured audit protocol will produce a more reliable, standardized, and ethically grounded method for validating the outputs and processes of AI-assisted scientific writing than current informal practices.
proposals.method: The research will develop a prototype audit framework by deconstructing the double-blind peer review workflow into core components (e.g., reviewer selection, evaluation criteria, conflict-of-interest management) and mapping them to analogous functions for AI tool assessment. This will involve creating specific evaluation rubrics for AI-generated text, focusing on accuracy, source attribution, and potential plagiarism using established similarity detection algorithms (e.g., BERT-based embeddings for semantic analysis). The framework will be tested via simulated audits on outputs from widely available large language models (e.g., GPT-4) using a corpus of published journal abstracts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, actionable procedural model for journals, institutions, and researchers to ethically validate AI writing assistants, moving beyond abstract principles. It will contribute a novel methodological bridge between traditional research integrity practices and emerging AI governance, offering a template for responsible innovation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has discussed AI ethics broadly or AI's role in peer review, this proposal uniquely inverts the relationship, systematically applying the mature, trusted *process of peer review itself* as the model for governing the new tool, creating a formal audit standard where none exists.

proposals.title: A Modular Framework for Bias-Audited Micro-Task AI in Scholarly Workflows: Towards Equitable Research Assistance
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI tools into scholarly research, from literature summarization to manuscript drafting, raises significant ethical concerns about embedded biases that can perpetuate inequities in knowledge production. Current guidance for AI in research, as noted in recent work, emphasizes the need for new ethical frameworks but lacks concrete, actionable methodologies for bias mitigation at the operational level. A monolithic approach to auditing complex AI systems is impractical and fails to provide researchers with the granular understanding needed to make informed, equitable choices about tool use in their specific workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: By decomposing research assistance into discrete micro-tasks and subjecting each to rigorous, task-specific algorithmic fairness audits, we can create a modular system where bias profiles are transparent, enabling researchers to construct AI-assisted workflows with explicit awareness and mitigation of potential biases at each step.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework comprising: 1) A suite of fine-tuned, specialized LLMs for defined micro-tasks (e.g., abstract generation, keyword suggestion, citation formatting). 2) For each micro-task model, we will implement and adapt pre-2026 bias auditing algorithms (e.g., AI Fairness 360 toolkit metrics, disaggregated evaluation across demographic and disciplinary subgroups) on relevant benchmark datasets. 3) We will create a metadata schema to document each tool's bias audit results, generating a 'bias profile card'. 4) A prototype platform will allow researchers to select and chain audited tools, with the system presenting aggregated bias disclosures for the constructed workflow.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, implementable standard for transparent and accountable micro-automation in research, moving beyond abstract ethical principles. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for AI ethics in science that empowers researchers as informed users, and offer concrete guidance to journals and publishers on evaluating AI-assisted submissions based on disclosed bias profiles of the tools used.
proposals.novelty: The approach shifts the paradigm from auditing monolithic AI systems to auditing constituent micro-tools, enabling precise bias understanding and combinatorial control within research workflows—a granularity not addressed in existing high-level ethical guidance for AI in research.

proposals.title: Detecting AI-Generated Text via Stylistic Entropy Loss: An Ethical Signal Processing Framework
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly writing necessitates robust, ethical detection tools to preserve research integrity and accountability. Current detection methods, often reliant on black-box classifiers or watermarking, raise ethical concerns about bias and transparency, and can be circumvented. A novel, interpretable approach is urgently needed to support journal editors and research institutions in screening submissions, as highlighted by recent calls for guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: AI text generation acts as a 'stylistic compressor,' systematically reducing the entropy and complexity of an author's unique writing signal. Therefore, quantifying this loss of stylistic entropy and other complexity metrics can reliably distinguish AI-generated text from human-authored text.
proposals.method: We will adapt signal processing metrics to quantify textual 'degradation.' For a corpus of human and AI-generated text pairs on identical prompts, we will compute entropy measures (e.g., Shannon entropy on syntactic POS tag sequences), lexical diversity (via Simpson's Index), and 'burstiness' of sentence structures. We will then train a transparent model (e.g., logistic regression or a simple neural network) on these interpretable features to classify text origin, benchmarking against state-of-the-art detectors (e.g., GPTZero, DetectGPT) as of 2025.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, interpretable detection framework grounded in signal processing theory, moving beyond pattern-matching to a principled model of stylistic loss. It will deliver concrete, ethically-aligned methodological guidance for journals and researchers, directly addressing the gap identified in Resnik's prior work on AI ethics and misconduct screening.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual analogy treating AI generation as a lossy compression filter for authorial style, a perspective not explored in pre-2026 NLP or AI ethics literature. This shifts the detection paradigm from seeking AI 'artifacts' to measuring the attenuation of a human signal.

proposals.title: Attributing Liability for AI-Induced Stylistic Degradation: A Forensic Framework for Legal and Scholarly Accountability
proposals.motivation: The widespread use of generative AI in scholarly and professional writing introduces new risks of error and harm due to the subtle degradation of authorial style, such as homogenization or the introduction of factual inconsistencies. Current ethical guidance for AI in research, which Resnik has contributed to, focuses on disclosure and misuse but lacks a forensic methodology to trace specific harms back to their source. This gap creates a significant challenge for legal systems and journal editors seeking to assign accountability when AI-generated text leads to professional, financial, or reputational damage.
proposals.hypothesis: Forensic linguistic analysis can isolate a quantifiable 'degradation footprint' in AI-assisted text that is sufficiently specific to attribute causal responsibility, either to the AI system's design (developer liability) or to the user's negligent prompting and deployment (user liability).
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-stage computational linguistics approach. First, we will use fine-tuned transformer models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) and stylometric feature sets (lexical richness, syntactic patterns, readability metrics) to establish a baseline 'stylistic signature' for human authors. Second, we will generate degraded text via controlled prompting of models like GPT-3/4 and Claude, then use attribution algorithms (including out-of-distribution detection and influence functions) to quantify the deviation from the human baseline and correlate specific degradation patterns to model architectures and prompt types.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, evidence-based framework for attributing liability in cases involving AI-generated text, directly informing legal standards of care and causation. It will also provide concrete methodological guidance for journals and institutions, as called for in Resnik's prior work, to forensically screen submissions and investigate potential misconduct linked to AI use.
proposals.novelty: While existing work detects AI use generically, this proposal uniquely seeks to quantify the *specific degradation* caused and forensically link it to a point in the chain of causation (developer vs. user), creating a new bridge between computational stylometry and legal liability theory.

proposals.title: An Immunological Framework for AI-Generated Text Detection: Developing a Self-Critiquing System for Scholarly Integrity
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scientific writing and publishing creates urgent ethical and practical challenges, including plagiarism, undisclosed authorship, and the erosion of trust in scholarly communication. Current detection tools are often reactive, unreliable against evolving models, and lack a principled framework for identifying subtle integrity violations. This research addresses the critical need, highlighted in recent bioethics literature, for scalable, proactive guidance to manage AI's role in research.
proposals.hypothesis: An AI critic system, trained on a controlled corpus of verified 'self' (human-authored) and 'non-self' (AI-generated/problematic) text using an immunological analogy, can learn to produce specialized 'critique vectors' that reliably identify characteristic artifacts and integrity violations in AI-assisted manuscripts.
proposals.method: We will construct a high-quality, domain-specific corpus of human-authored journal submissions and synthetic text from models like GPT-4. Using a contrastive learning framework (e.g., SimCLR or a siamese network architecture), we will train a transformer-based encoder (e.g., BERT or RoBERTa) to map texts into an embedding space where 'self' and 'non-self' clusters are maximally separated. The system's internal representations will be probed to extract and interpret 'critique vectors'—directions in the latent space that correspond to specific AI-generated stylistic, logical, or ethical flaws.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically grounded technical tool for journals and institutions to screen AI-generated content. It will also contribute a new conceptual framework—the immunological analogy—to the ethics of AI in research, moving beyond simple detection to a model of learned, specific critique that can inform author guidance and policy development.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of immunological self/non-self discrimination principles to AI text analysis, moving beyond statistical detection to a model that learns to produce interpretable 'antibody-like' critique vectors. This bridges a gap between technical machine learning and the conceptual bioethics of research integrity.

proposals.title: Defining the Boundaries of Human Oversight: A Framework for Assessing the Acceptability of Fully Automated Editorial Processes in Scholarly Publishing
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI tools into scholarly publishing, from screening for misconduct to peer review, has triggered a near-universal ethical and practical insistence on maintaining human oversight. This principle, while precautionary, risks becoming a dogma that stifles innovation and fails to account for scenarios where automation might be superior in objectivity, scalability, or efficiency. A rigorous investigation is needed to move beyond blanket mandates and provide nuanced guidance for publishers and editors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exist specific, well-defined classes of editorial tasks and manuscript types for which a fully automated process, governed by a validated algorithmic framework, can meet or exceed the ethical and quality standards of human-managed oversight.
proposals.method: The research will employ a multi-method approach. First, a conceptual analysis will deconstruct 'editorial oversight' into discrete tasks (e.g., plagiarism detection, formatting compliance, statistical checklist verification). Second, we will conduct scenario-based thought experiments and Delphi surveys with editors, authors, and ethicists to identify candidate tasks for full automation. Third, we will model potential automated systems using state-of-the-art, pre-2026 AI architectures (e.g., transformer-based models for semantic similarity checking, rule-based expert systems for methodological checks) to define the technical and validation requirements for acceptable deployment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first structured ethical framework for evaluating when human oversight can be responsibly circumscribed in scholarly publishing. It will offer concrete, actionable guidance to journals and a defensible counterpoint to the prevailing 'human-in-the-loop' imperative, grounded in the specific capabilities and limitations of pre-2026 AI technology.
proposals.novelty: While existing literature calls for human oversight of AI in research, this proposal directly challenges that axiom by systematically investigating its exceptions, shifting the debate from whether to automate to under what precise conditions automation is acceptable.

proposals.title: Ethical and Practical Frameworks for Fully Automated AI Peer Review: A Pilot Study in Computational Science
proposals.motivation: Current discourse on AI in peer review focuses on augmentation, yet the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) like GPT-4 necessitates a proactive ethical and practical analysis of fully autonomous systems. This shift from 'human-in-the-loop' to 'AI-on-the-loop' presents novel risks, including the entrenchment of AI biases at scale, the loss of human judgment for novel or interdisciplinary work, and the potential for systemic manipulation. A structured investigation is urgently needed to guide publishers and funders before such systems are deployed.
proposals.hypothesis: That a structured, multi-model AI system can be designed to perform the core functions of peer review (validity, significance, clarity assessment) with reliability comparable to human reviewers for well-defined scientific domains, but that its ethical and epistemic limitations will require novel governance frameworks distinct from those for human or augmented review.
proposals.method: We will design a prototype system using a pipeline of specialized LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 2) and transformer-based classifiers. A 'decomposer' model will extract claims and methodology; a 'validator' model will cross-reference these against a curated database of published literature (e.g., via embeddings); and a 'critic' model will assess novelty and clarity. The system will be piloted on a corpus of 500 pre-2025 published and deliberately flawed manuscripts from arXiv's cs.* categories. Performance will be benchmarked against human reviewer decisions and reports from the same papers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a functional prototype demonstrating the technical feasibility and failure modes of autonomous review; 2) a structured ethical analysis of the risks, including bias amplification, accountability gaps, and impacts on scientific creativity; 3) evidence-based preliminary guidance for journals and conferences considering such systems, extending current AI ethics frameworks into the domain of scholarly evaluation.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond the prevalent 'augmentation' paradigm to critically assess replacement, and it uniquely integrates a technical pilot with a parallel ethical governance analysis, reflecting Resnik's expertise in research ethics. This dual approach is absent from pre-2026 literature, which primarily discusses AI as a tool for human reviewers.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Impact of Large Language Model-Assisted Review on Feedback Quality and Editorial Bias: A Randomized Controlled Experiment
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI tools like GPT-4 into scholarly workflows, including peer review, is outpacing ethical and empirical guidance. Journals and publishers are actively considering AI for screening and review assistance, but there is a critical lack of evidence on how such tools affect the depth, accuracy, and potential biases of reviewer feedback, which are core to maintaining research integrity. This study addresses the urgent need for data-driven guidance called for in recent literature on AI ethics in science.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reviewers using LLM assistance will produce longer and more structured feedback but may exhibit reduced critical depth in methodological critique and introduce new, systematic biases related to the AI's training data and prompting, potentially influencing editorial decisions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-arm, blinded randomized controlled trial with active researchers as participants.
