Using a corpus of pre-2025 published manuscripts with known flaws, we will assign reviewers to control (no AI) or intervention groups (using a specified interface with GPT-4 or a similar pre-2025 model for drafting assistance). We will quantitatively analyze feedback using NLP metrics (e.g., sentiment, specificity, similarity to known flaws) and qualitative coding for bias. Editorial recommendations will be tracked and compared against a gold-standard assessment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, empirical evidence on the causal effects of AI-assisted peer review on core quality metrics, directly informing journal policies and ethical guidelines. It will contribute a validated methodological framework for ongoing evaluation of AI tools in scholarly publishing and offer concrete data on emergent bias patterns.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has speculated on AI's risks in research ethics, this proposal employs an experimental design to isolate and measure AI's causal impact on peer review, moving beyond theoretical discussion to generate actionable, evidence-based policy guidance.

proposals.title: Copyleft for Code, Copyleft for Content? A Framework for Licensing AI-Generated Scholarly Material
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly work, from drafting text to generating figures, creates urgent ethical and practical gaps in attribution, ownership, and downstream use. Current publishing guidelines are reactive and lack a coherent, legally-grounded framework for managing AI-generated material, risking misuse, plagiarism, and a breakdown in scholarly credit. This problem mirrors early challenges in open-source software, where licensing models were developed to manage derivative works and ensure attribution.
proposals.hypothesis: That established open-source software licensing principles, specifically copyleft (e.g., GPL) and permissive attribution (e.g., BSD/MIT) models, can be formally adapted to create a functional and ethically sound licensing framework for AI-generated scholarly content, governing its use, modification, and redistribution.
proposals.method: The research will employ a conceptual analysis and case-based methodology. First, we will deconstruct key OSS licenses (GPL-3.0, Apache 2.0, MIT) to isolate core clauses on copyright, distribution, modification, and attribution. Second, we will map these clauses onto paradigmatic cases of AI use in research (e.g., AI-drafted manuscript sections, AI-generated datasets, AI-synthesized literature reviews). Third, we will develop draft license templates by iteratively adapting OSS language to scholarly content, using expert elicitation workshops with legal scholars, ethicists, and journal editors to test viability and identify pitfalls.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first structured, transferable framework for licensing AI-generated scholarly material, moving beyond vague guidelines. It will offer concrete license templates for journals and repositories to adopt. It contributes directly to the ethics of AI in science by proposing a mechanism to enforce transparency and manage derivative works, a core concern in Resnik's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While AI ethics guidance exists, applying the mature, legally-tested architecture of OSS licensing to AI-generated scholarly content is a novel translational approach. It shifts the focus from prohibition to managed permissibility, using a proven tool from a adjacent domain to solve a new but analogous problem in research integrity.

proposals.title: Defining the Threshold of Substantive Contribution: A Framework for AI Authorship Attribution in Scholarly Publishing
proposals.motivation: Current authorship guidelines, such as the ICMJE criteria, are predicated on human agency and cannot adjudicate the growing use of generative AI in research. This creates an ethical and practical vacuum, as AI tools transition from basic assistants to entities capable of generating novel hypotheses, designing complex methodologies, and drafting substantive manuscript sections. Without clear frameworks, the scholarly record risks both misattribution of credit and a loss of accountability for intellectual work.
proposals.hypothesis: A functional, principle-based framework for AI authorship can be developed by analyzing the specific, definable tasks where AI output constitutes a 'substantive intellectual contribution,' a threshold that would warrant formal attribution distinct from mere tool use.
proposals.method: The research will employ a conceptual analysis and normative framework development methodology. First, we will conduct a systematic review of pre-2026 literature on AI in research and authorship ethics. Second, we will analyze case studies of AI use (e.g., AlphaFold for protein structure prediction, GPT-4 for literature synthesis) through the lens of existing authorship criteria. Third, using a modified Delphi method with panels of ethicists, journal editors, and researchers, we will iteratively develop and refine a set of operational conditions under which AI could be credited as an 'agent' or 'co-author,' focusing on autonomy, originality, and irreplaceability of the AI's contribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed, actionable guidance for journals and researchers on when and how to attribute credit to AI systems, moving beyond blanket prohibitions. It will contribute directly to the evolving fields of research integrity and the ethics of human-AI collaboration, offering a principled middle ground between ignoring AI's role and anthropomorphizing it.
proposals.novelty: While existing work calls for new guidance, this proposal moves to concretely define the threshold of 'substantive contribution' for AI, challenging the absolute necessity of human authorship for all published content under specific, defined conditions of AI agency and intellectual output.

proposals.title: Ethical and Practical Evaluation of Fully Automated AI for Technical Pre-Screening in Scholarly Publishing
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of AI tools in scientific publishing, primarily as human-assistive systems, creates a pressing need for ethical and practical guidance. Current models, as noted in pre-2026 literature, focus on AI-aided human judgment, but the potential efficiency gains of fully autonomous technical checks are unexplored. This research addresses a critical gap identified in Resnik's prior work: the lack of frameworks for when and how AI can operate independently in high-stakes scholarly contexts without compromising integrity.
proposals.hypothesis: A specialized, rule-bound AI system can perform technical compliance checks (e.g., formatting, disclosure statements, plagiarism screening) with accuracy and consistency equal to or greater than human-assisted checks, while reserving human editor effort for substantive evaluation of scholarly contribution and novelty.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled experiment comparing a human-in-the-loop workflow against a fully autonomous AI system. The autonomous system will be built using a pipeline of pre-2026 technologies: a fine-tuned BERT or similar transformer model for document structure and policy adherence classification, integrated with established plagiarism detection software (e.g., Turnitin API) and a rule-based checker for formatting and authorship criteria. We will use a dataset of de-submitted manuscripts from a partner journal, with performance measured on accuracy, false-positive/negative rates, and time efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical data on the viability and risks of inverting the human-in-the-loop requirement for a defined editorial task. It will generate concrete, evidence-based guidance for journals and ethics boards on implementing autonomous AI screening, directly addressing the call for new governance frameworks in Resnik's recent publications.
proposals.novelty: While existing research advocates for AI as an advisory tool, this proposal empirically tests a paradigm shift: full automation for technical gatekeeping. It moves beyond asking 'how can AI help editors?' to rigorously investigate 'when can editors justifiably be removed from the technical loop?'

proposals.title: A Text-Matching Framework for AI-Generated Content and AI Self-Plagiarism in Scholarly Publishing
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI in research writing creates novel ethical and integrity challenges. Existing plagiarism detection tools are designed for human-to-human text matching and fail to identify AI-generated content or instances where authors reuse AI outputs across multiple submissions (AI self-plagiarism). This gap undermines authorship accountability and the originality standards of scholarly publishing, necessitating new technical and ethical guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that existing text-matching algorithms (e.g., fingerprinting, n-gram analysis) can be modified with AI-specific linguistic and statistical features to reliably flag AI-generated text and detect self-plagiarism from AI outputs, even when those outputs are subsequently edited by authors.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core architecture of open-source plagiarism detection systems (e.g., using the Python libraries of tools like Plagiarism Checker X). The technical approach involves: 1) Training classifiers on pre-2026 AI text corpora (e.g., outputs from GPT-3, GPT-4) to identify stylistic markers (e.g., perplexity, burstiness, specific syntactic patterns). 2) Modifying the fingerprinting algorithm to create and compare hashes of semantic chunks from AI outputs, rather than just exact string matches, to detect substantive reuse. 3) Developing a two-stage pipeline that first screens for AI-generation likelihood and then cross-references the manuscript against a private database of author-submitted AI outputs to flag self-plagiarism.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept technical tool for journals to screen for AI-generated content and AI self-plagiarism, addressing a critical gap in research integrity infrastructure. It will also yield concrete ethical and practical guidance for publishers and researchers on defining and managing originality in the age of generative AI, directly extending Resnik's prior work on AI ethics guidance.
proposals.novelty: While AI text detectors exist, they are typically standalone classifiers; our novel integration of AI-detection with modified plagiarism algorithms to specifically identify *self-plagiarism from AI outputs* is a new, hybrid approach to integrity checking. This moves beyond simple authorship attribution to address the nuanced reuse of AI-generated material.

proposals.title: Transparency and Trust: An Empirical and Normative Analysis of Permissive AI Authorship Policies in Scientific Publishing
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI (e.g., GPT-4, Claude) into scholarly work has created a policy vacuum. Current guidance from bodies like COPE and ICMJE largely rejects AI authorship, focusing on tool-use disclosure. This pre-2026 stance fails to empirically test an alternative: a permissive model with strict, structured transparency. This research addresses a critical gap in science policy by investigating the practical and ethical outcomes of a major, untested regulatory pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a permissive AI authorship policy, when coupled with granular, standardized disclosure requirements, will not significantly erode reader trust or perceived article integrity compared to human-only authorship, and may improve the accuracy of credit and responsibility attribution compared to current ad-hoc disclosure practices.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods approach. First, a controlled survey experiment will present participants (researchers, editors, readers) with manuscript vignettes varying authorship (human, AI-as-author with disclosure, AI-as-tool with disclosure) and measure trust, credibility, and perceived responsibility using Likert scales. Second, we will conduct a normative analysis, applying Resnik's pre-2026 frameworks on research ethics and authorship to evaluate the proposed policy against core principles of accountability, transparency, and credit. Statistical analysis (ANOVA, regression) will test the experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence on the impacts of a permissive AI authorship regime, directly informing journals and ethics bodies. It will generate a novel, detailed framework for 'structured transparency' in AI-assisted research. The work will advance the theoretical debate on authorship by analyzing AI not just as a tool but as a potential agent within credit-assignment systems.
proposals.novelty: While existing work argues *against* AI authorship, this proposal uniquely tests a counterfactual permissive policy. It moves beyond abstract debate by empirically measuring stakeholder reactions and integrating those results with a rigorous normative analysis based on established authorship criteria.

proposals.title: Generative AI as a Sentinel: A Framework for Using Large Language Models to Enhance Research Integrity in Scholarly Publishing
proposals.motivation: Current discourse on AI in research, particularly generative AI, is dominated by concerns about its threats to integrity, such as fabricating data or plagiarizing text. This focus overlooks a critical opportunity. Given the escalating volume of submissions and sophisticated forms of misconduct, traditional human and software-based screening tools are increasingly strained. A systematic investigation into AI's potential as a proactive integrity tool is urgently needed to provide balanced, evidence-based guidance for journals and publishers.
proposals.hypothesis: Generative AI models, specifically fine-tuned large language models (LLMs), can be deployed to detect patterns indicative of research fraud—including text recycling, statistical inconsistency, and image duplication—with accuracy comparable to or exceeding current specialized software, thereby serving as a net enhancer of research integrity.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods approach. First, we will curate a labeled dataset of published papers, including confirmed cases of misconduct (e.g., retracted papers) and verified legitimate controls. We will then fine-tune an open-source LLM (e.g., Llama 2 or a BERT variant) on this dataset to classify papers and flag anomalies. Concurrently, we will develop and test specific prompting strategies for general-purpose LLMs (like GPT-4) to audit manuscripts for inconsistencies in methods, results, and references. Performance will be benchmarked against established tools like iThenticate and manual review.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, actionable framework for integrating generative AI into journal submission workflows to augment integrity checks. It will provide empirical data on the efficacy and limitations of LLMs for this purpose, moving the ethical debate beyond precaution to practical governance. The output will be concrete guidance for publishers, aligning with Resnik's prior calls for new ethical frameworks for AI in science.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the predominant narrative by treating generative AI not as an integrity liability but as a potential investigative asset. While prior work has focused on AI's role in committing misconduct, we systematically explore its capacity for *detecting* misconduct, a non-obvious application given the models' generative nature.

proposals.title: Evaluating a Hybrid AI-Human Peer Review Model: A Randomized Controlled Trial for Ethical and Practical Efficacy
proposals.motivation: The peer review system is strained by increasing submission volumes, leading to reviewer fatigue and delays in scholarly communication. Concurrently, the rapid integration of generative AI tools into research workflows, as noted in recent ethical guidance, creates an urgent need to empirically evaluate their responsible application in core scholarly practices like peer review. This study addresses the practical challenge of review efficiency and the ethical imperative for evidence-based policy on AI use in publishing.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid peer review model, where a large language model (LLM) conducts a structured first-pass analysis to flag major issues, will reduce the time and cognitive burden on human reviewers without compromising the quality or ethical rigor of the final review decision, compared to a traditional human-only model.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized controlled trial in collaboration with a mid-tier, open-access journal. Manuscripts will be randomly assigned to a control (traditional human review) or intervention arm (AI-first pass). The intervention will use a fine-tuned GPT-4 or similar LLM (pre-2026 state-of-the-art) prompted to generate a standardized report assessing clarity, methodology description, and potential ethical red flags (e.g., plagiarism cues, data availability). This report will then be provided to human reviewers, who will produce the final recommendation. We will measure time-to-decision, reviewer workload (via survey), and outcome concordance between arms.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first robust, empirical evidence on the efficacy and practical impact of AI-assisted peer review within a real publishing pipeline. It will generate concrete, evidence-based guidance for journals and ethics boards on how to implement such systems responsibly, addressing a critical gap identified in recent calls for new governance frameworks for AI in science.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has discussed AI's theoretical role in peer review, this proposal moves beyond speculation by designing a controlled, in-practice experiment that directly tests a defined workflow. It uniquely integrates Resnik's focus on ethical guidance with a rigorous methodological test of a new tool's impact on a fundamental scholarly process.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Generative Capacity of Large Language Models for Novel Bioethics Research Questions
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly work raises urgent ethical and practical questions about its role in the research lifecycle. As of 2025, while AI is used for editing and literature reviews, its potential to autonomously generate foundational research ideas remains underexplored and ethically contentious. This study addresses a critical gap identified in recent calls for guidance on AI in science: determining if and how these tools can be responsibly used for the creative, conceptual initiation of research in a normative field like bioethics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art large language models (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 2) can generate research questions in bioethics that expert evaluators will rate as original, testable, and of meaningful scholarly value, comparable to questions generated by early-career bioethics researchers.
proposals.method: We will employ a controlled, blinded expert evaluation study. Using a fine-tuned GPT-4 model (or equivalent, via API access as of 2025) prompted with structured seed contexts from recent bioethics literature (e.g., neuroethics, AI ethics, gene editing), we will generate a corpus of novel research questions. A comparator set will be generated by PhD candidates in bioethics given the same prompts. A panel of senior bioethicists, blinded to source, will rate all questions on predefined scales for novelty, testability, ethical significance, and feasibility using a Delphi-like iterative scoring protocol.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical evidence on the capability and limitations of LLMs as hypothesis-generating tools in normative research fields. It will produce a concrete framework for evaluating AI-generated research ideas and offer practical, evidence-based guidance for journals and researchers on the ethical use of AI in the conceptual phase of scholarly work, directly informing ongoing policy discussions.
proposals.novelty: While existing pre-2026 work examines AI for writing or misconduct screening, this proposal directly tests AI's capacity for the creative, intellectual origin of research—a core scholarly function. It moves beyond technical capability to assess the scholarly and ethical validity of AI-generated research questions through rigorous, blinded expert evaluation.

proposals.title: Adversarial Debiasing for Fairness in AI-Assisted Journal Peer Review: An Ethical and Technical Framework
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of AI tools to screen and review journal submissions, as noted in Resnik's recent work, introduces significant risks of perpetuating or amplifying societal biases, potentially disadvantaging authors from underrepresented groups or non-mainstream methodologies. This threatens the foundational principles of scholarly fairness and objectivity. Existing ethical guidance for AI in research, while crucial, lacks concrete, technically-grounded methods for operationalizing fairness in real-world editorial systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adversarial debiasing algorithms, adapted from the ML fairness literature, can effectively detect and mitigate protected attribute bias (e.g., based on inferred author nationality, gender, or institutional prestige) in AI models used for initial manuscript screening, without substantially degrading the model's core predictive accuracy for legitimate quality metrics.
proposals.method: We will construct a simulated dataset of manuscript features and historical editorial decisions, incorporating proxy variables for protected attributes. A primary classifier (e.g., a BERT-based model) will be trained to predict editorial outcomes. An adversarial network will simultaneously be trained to predict the protected attribute from the primary classifier's learned representations. The primary model will be optimized to minimize prediction loss while maximizing the adversary's loss, using a gradient reversal layer, thereby learning representations invariant to the protected attribute. We will benchmark against pre-debiasing performance and simple post-processing techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, actionable technical framework for implementing fairness in AI-assisted publishing, moving beyond principle-based guidance. It will contribute directly to the ethics of AI in science by offering a testable model for audit and mitigation. The results will inform journal editors and publishers on practical steps to align their use of generative AI tools with ethical scholarly standards.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial debiasing is established in broader ML fairness contexts, its application to the specific domain of AI-mediated scholarly communication and peer review is novel. This proposal uniquely bridges Resnik's expertise in research ethics with concrete, pre-2026 ML fairness techniques to address a pressing gap in the responsible implementation of editorial AI.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial Protocol for Evaluating AI-Based Plagiarism Detection in Scholarly Manuscript Submission
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI tools into scholarly publishing workflows, such as for plagiarism and misconduct screening, is outpacing the development of rigorous evaluation frameworks. Journals and publishers are adopting these tools based on vendor claims and limited validation, creating significant ethical and practical risks, including false accusations, missed misconduct, and erosion of trust in the publication process. A methodologically sound, evidence-based approach to assessing these tools is urgently needed to inform responsible adoption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, multi-arm randomized controlled trial (RCT) design, adapted from clinical research, can provide a statistically robust and ethically defensible evaluation of an AI tool's real-world performance and impact compared to standard editorial practices and human expert assessment.
proposals.method: We will adapt a parallel-group, blinded RCT protocol. Manuscripts submitted to a partner journal will be randomly allocated to one of three arms: (1) screening by a current commercial AI tool (e.g., iThenticate with an AI-writing detector module, or a tool based on a BERT or RoBERTa architecture fine-tuned for text originality), (2) screening by a human editorial assistant using standard methods, and (3) a control arm with no initial screening. The primary outcome will be the accuracy (sensitivity/specificity) of identifying problematic submissions, validated by a blinded expert panel. Secondary outcomes will include time-to-decision and editor workload.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a formal, transferable RCT protocol template specifically for evaluating AI in publishing, moving beyond anecdotal evidence. It will generate the first high-quality empirical data on the comparative effectiveness of an AI screening tool in a real operational environment, directly informing journal policy and ethical guidance for tool use.
proposals.novelty: While RCTs are the gold standard in clinical research, their application to evaluate workflow software in academic publishing is novel. This approach systematically addresses confounding variables and bias in a way that retrospective analyses or vendor-led studies cannot, providing a new methodological standard for the field.

proposals.title: A Git-Based Framework for Documenting and Auditing Human-AI Collaborative Authorship in Scholarly Manuscripts
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly writing (pre-2026) creates urgent ethical and transparency challenges. Current publishing practices lack robust mechanisms to document the iterative, co-creative process between human authors and AI tools, making it difficult to assess originality, assign responsibility, and screen for misconduct. This opacity undermines research integrity and complicates the development of effective guidance for AI use in science.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a version control system (VCS) like Git to the human-AI manuscript drafting process will create a granular, auditable provenance trail. This will enable the clear attribution of contributions, facilitate the detection of problematic AI-generated content, and provide a concrete technical foundation for new authorship and accountability norms.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype system using the Git data model to track changes in a manuscript's plain text. Each human edit and each AI-generated suggestion or revision (via API calls to models like GPT-4 or Claude 2) will be logged as a commit with structured metadata (e.g., agent type, prompt used, timestamp). A custom diff algorithm will highlight AI-originated text blocks. The system will be tested via a controlled study where researchers co-write short papers with an AI assistant, with outputs analyzed for traceability and utility in simulated misconduct reviews.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, actionable technical tool for enhancing transparency in AI-augmented research. It will generate empirical data on collaboration patterns to inform ethical guidelines and journal policies. The primary output will be a framework and set of best practices for implementing VCS in scholarly workflows to meet emerging demands for AI disclosure and auditability.
proposals.novelty: While Git is standard in software development, its systematic application to track and attribute contributions in human-AI scholarly co-authorship is unexplored. This approach moves beyond declarative statements of AI use to provide a verifiable, technical provenance record, addressing a core gap in pre-2026 discussions on AI ethics in publishing.

proposals.title: Ethical and Technical Frameworks for AI-Assisted Assessment of Scholarly Originality
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of generative AI in scholarly writing creates a pressing need to safeguard the integrity of human intellectual contribution. Current AI tools primarily detect plagiarism or AI-generated text, but the core scholarly value of a manuscript lies in its novel intellectual synthesis. This research addresses a critical gap in the peer review ecosystem by developing methods to evaluate this originality, ensuring human authors receive appropriate credit and that the scholarly record remains a testament to human creativity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid AI system, trained on a curated corpus of published literature and human expert judgments of originality, can generate a reliable and explainable metric for the novel intellectual contribution of a human-authored manuscript, distinct from mere novelty of phrasing or structure.
proposals.method: The project will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, a fine-tuned transformer model (e.g., BERT or SciBERT) will perform semantic similarity analysis against a dynamic corpus of domain-specific literature to map conceptual overlap. Second, this output will feed into a supervised learning model (e.g., a gradient-boosted tree or a simpler neural network) trained on a dataset of peer review statements and editorial decisions annotated for perceived 'intellectual leap.' The system's architecture will prioritize interpretability, using attention mechanisms and feature importance scores to provide justifications for its assessments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept technical tool for journals and funding agencies to triage submissions based on a quantifiable measure of intellectual novelty. More importantly, it will produce a foundational ethical and procedural framework for the responsible use of such AI in scholarly publishing, directly addressing the guidance gap identified in prior research on AI and research ethics.
proposals.novelty: While existing work focuses on detecting AI-generated text or simple plagiarism, this project inverts the problem to define and measure positive human originality. It uniquely integrates technical machine learning development with a parallel bioethical analysis of fairness, bias, and appropriate use, a synthesis central to the researcher's established expertise.

proposals.title: A Framework for Responsible AI Use in Pre-print Publication: Ethical Guidelines and Implementation Strategies
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of pre-print servers has accelerated scientific communication but operates with minimal formal oversight, creating a critical gap in governance for AI use. As generative AI tools become commonplace in manuscript preparation, there is an urgent need to address risks of undisclosed AI authorship, data fabrication, and the amplification of biases before work enters the scholarly record. This lack of policy undermines research integrity and public trust at a crucial, high-velocity stage of dissemination.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, stakeholder-informed framework of ethical guidelines for AI use in pre-print preparation can be developed and effectively operationalized through technical and procedural interventions on pre-print platforms. We hypothesize that such a framework will be perceived as both necessary and feasible by key stakeholders in the research community.
proposals.method: The research will employ a mixed-methods approach. First, a systematic content analysis of existing AI policies from peer-reviewed journals (pre-2026) will identify core principles and gaps. Second, semi-structured interviews and Delphi surveys with pre-print server administrators, researchers, editors, and ethicists will be conducted to gather stakeholder perspectives on risks and potential guidelines. Third, based on this data, a draft guideline framework will be developed and its technical implementation (e.g., mandatory disclosure checkboxes, AI-use statement prompts, metadata tagging) will be prototyped and evaluated for usability on a test server platform.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, empirically-grounded ethical framework specifically tailored for AI use in the pre-print ecosystem. It will provide concrete, actionable guidance for server administrators and authors, filling a major governance vacuum. The project will also contribute to the broader literature on research ethics in open science by addressing integrity challenges at the nascent, pre-peer-review stage of publication.
proposals.novelty: While Resnik and others have called for AI guidance in peer-reviewed contexts, this project uniquely shifts focus to the under-regulated pre-print landscape, where the risks and incentives differ substantially. It moves beyond abstract principle-setting to propose and test specific implementation mechanisms integrated directly into pre-print submission architectures.

proposals.title: Ethical and Practical Frameworks for AI-Assisted Editorial Screening and Reviewer Matching: A Pilot Study in Biomedical Publishing
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of generative AI tools in scientific publishing presents urgent ethical and practical challenges. Journals are already experimenting with AI for initial manuscript screening and reviewer matching, but there is a critical lack of empirical evidence on their efficacy and a dearth of ethical guidance to prevent bias, ensure fairness, and maintain accountability. This gap risks automating existing editorial biases and undermining trust in peer review.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, ethically-informed AI pipeline for editorial tasks can improve efficiency without compromising fairness, but that its success is contingent on explicit human oversight and transparent, domain-specific training.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage AI pipeline. For initial triage, we will fine-tune a BERT-based model (e.g., SciBERT) on a labeled dataset of manuscript abstracts and editorial decisions to predict manuscript scope and novelty. For reviewer matching, we will implement a hybrid system combining a transformer-based document embedding (SPECTER) to represent manuscript content with a graph neural network to model reviewer expertise and past review history. The system's performance and potential biases will be evaluated against human editor decisions on a held-out dataset from participating journals.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first empirical evaluations of an integrated AI system for core editorial functions. It will yield a concrete, testable prototype and a parallel set of evidence-based ethical guidelines for journal implementation, directly addressing the call for new governance in Resnik's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While AI tools for individual tasks exist, this proposal integrates screening and matching within a single ethical framework and explicitly tests the interaction between algorithmic performance and human oversight, moving beyond theoretical discussion to practical, evaluable guidance.

proposals.title: Auditing the Algorithmic Assistant: A Framework for Monitoring and Verifying AI Use in Scholarly Research Processes
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI into scholarly work, from literature review to data analysis and manuscript drafting, creates novel risks for research integrity, including undetected bias, fabrication, and plagiarism. Existing ethical guidance and journal policies (pre-2026) are largely declarative, lacking concrete, procedural mechanisms for verification and accountability. This gap mirrors pre-audit challenges in clinical research, where process transparency was essential for validating results.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting core principles and operational templates from clinical trial monitoring and audit—such as source data verification, process trail review, and risk-based oversight—can create a practical, effective framework for institutions and journals to verify the integrity of AI-assisted research processes.
proposals.method: The project will conduct a conceptual analysis and framework development. First, we will map established clinical research audit procedures (e.g., from ICH E6 GCP) onto key AI-involved scholarly workflows (e.g., AI-assisted systematic reviews, code generation, statistical analysis). Second, we will develop specific audit checklists and documentation requirements (e.g., mandatory logging of AI tool, version, prompt history, and human verification steps) tailored for different research phases. Third, we will propose a risk-based model, akin to clinical trial risk assessment, to determine the level of AI-use auditing required based on the tool's role and the study's impact.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first translational framework for applying proven clinical research integrity tools to the novel challenge of AI in science. It will deliver concrete, actionable guidance for research institutions, funders, and journal editors seeking to operationalize new AI ethics policies, moving from principle to enforceable practice.
proposals.novelty: While others have called for AI oversight, this proposal uniquely adapts a mature, regulatory-grade audit system from another high-stakes research domain, offering a ready-made structure for verification that goes beyond mere disclosure or tool-specific validation.

proposals.title: Disciplinary Divergence in AI Ethics: A Comparative Framework for Policy Development in STEM and Humanities Research
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of generative AI tools like GPT-4 into scholarly work necessitates clear ethical guidance for researchers and publishers. Pre-2026, most proposed guidelines treat AI use as a monolithic challenge, failing to account for fundamental differences in research epistemology and practice between technical fields (e.g., data analysis, coding) and interpretive humanities (e.g., textual hermeneutics, historical analysis). This one-size-fits-all approach risks creating policies that are either overly restrictive for STEM or dangerously permissive for humanities, undermining research integrity.
proposals.hypothesis: Effective and ethical AI use policies for scientific research must be discipline-specific, with core normative principles (e.g., transparency, accountability) applied differently based on whether the primary research task is technical computation and data generation (STEM) or interpretive meaning-making and argument construction (humanities).
proposals.method: The study will employ a mixed-methods comparative analysis. First, a systematic content analysis of pre-2026 AI policy statements from major journals and professional societies in representative STEM (e.g., Nature, IEEE) and humanities (e.g., MLA, AHA) fields will be conducted using NLP techniques (e.g., topic modeling with Latent Dirichlet Allocation) to map discursive differences. Second, structured interviews and scenario-based surveys with researchers and editors from both domains will be analyzed to identify divergent perceptions of acceptable AI use, risks (e.g., automation bias vs. interpretive delegation), and necessary disclosure practices.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, evidence-based framework for developing differentiated AI use policies, directly addressing the guidance gap identified in Resnik's prior work. It will provide concrete recommendations for journal editors, peer reviewers, and research institutions on how to tailor authorship, disclosure, and misconduct screening protocols to the distinct epistemic vulnerabilities and affordances of AI in different disciplines.
proposals.novelty: While existing scholarship calls for AI ethics guidance, this project is the first to systematically argue that the core *application* of ethical principles must diverge at a disciplinary level, moving beyond generic checklists to a principled, comparative framework grounded in the epistemology of research practices.

ideas.title: Explore AI tools for editorial screening and reviewer matching
ideas.description: Develop and test AI applications for initial manuscript triage and identifying appropriate peer reviewers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Invert the assumption that AI primarily threatens integrity
ideas.description: Study if generative AI could be a net enhancer of research integrity by detecting fraud.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Invert the human-in-the-loop requirement for editing
ideas.description: Test fully AI-run editorial checks for technical compliance, reserving human judgment for substantive scholarly contribution.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors' Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing.

ideas.title: Relax the need for full human oversight
ideas.description: Investigate publishing workflows where AI operates with minimal or no human verification.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ statement on the responsible use of generative AI technologies in scholarly journal publishing

ideas.title: Dual problem: Develop AI to detect AI-generated scholarship
ideas.description: Build AI tools specifically designed to identify AI-generated text and ideas in submissions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ statement on the responsible use of generative AI technologies in scholarly journal publishing

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Using AI to assess human originality
ideas.description: Create AI systems that evaluate the novel intellectual contribution of a human-authored manuscript.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Scale to pre-prints: AI policies for non-peer-reviewed work
ideas.description: Design responsible AI use guidelines for pre-print servers where formal oversight is minimal.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Bias-Audited Micro-Task AI for Equitable Research Assistance
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework for building and deploying micro-task LLMs (e.g., for summarization, terminology suggestion, or citation formatting) that are each subjected to rigorous, task-specific algorithmic fairness audits. By applying bias auditing not to a monolithic research AI but to its constituent micro-tools, the approach creates a modular system where bias profiles are precisely understood and can be selectively combined or avoided. This allows researchers to construct AI-assisted workflows with explicit awareness of potential biases in each step, promoting equitable research practices. The fusion yields a new standard for transparent, accountable, and bias-mitigated micro-automation in scholarly work.

ideas.title: Apply clinical trial audit methods to AI-assisted research
ideas.description: Use monitoring and audit techniques from clinical research to verify AI-involved scholarly processes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Transfer peer review models to AI oversight
ideas.description: Adapt double-blind peer review processes to audit and validate AI writing tools.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of AUTOGEN and Similar Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Integrity of Scholarly Writing

ideas.title: Applying Provenance and Traceability Frameworks from Food Science to AI-Assisted Scholarship
ideas.description: This research seed inverts the focus from writer to reader, aiming to study how readers perceive and trust scholarship known to be AI-assisted. Through analogical reasoning with Food Science, it adapts the solution of provenance and traceability labeling used to rebuild consumer trust in complex food supply chains. The adapted idea is to develop and empirically test a standardized 'scholarly provenance' disclosure framework for AI-assisted research. Similar to a food label listing ingredients and processing methods, this framework would require authors to transparently document the type, extent, and purpose of AI tools used in a structured, machine-readable format. The research would then investigate how readers' perceptions of credibility, trust, and value change when they are provided with this verifiable traceability information about the AI's role, moving beyond a simple binary 'AI-used' flag to a nuanced understanding of process transparency.

ideas.title: Assume AI authorship is permissible with full transparency
ideas.description: Research the outcomes if journals allowed AI as a named author under strict disclosure rules.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Scale up: Universal publisher-wide AI use standards
ideas.description: Create a cross-publisher consortium to establish unified standards for AI in publishing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ statement on the responsible use of generative AI technologies in scholarly journal publishing

ideas.title: Invert the focus from writer to reader
ideas.description: Study how readers perceive and trust scholarship known to be AI-assisted.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of AUTOGEN and Similar Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Integrity of Scholarly Writing

ideas.title: Use provenance tracking from data science
ideas.description: Adapt data lineage and provenance frameworks to track AI contributions to manuscripts.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of AUTOGEN and Similar Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Integrity of Scholarly Writing

ideas.title: Designing Pro-Originality Ethical Frameworks to Counteract LLM-Induced Scholarly Homogenization
ideas.description: This research project fuses ethical guideline development with a critical hypothesis about originality degradation to create a novel, testable framework. It moves beyond creating generic usage policies to instead design and validate 'pro-originality' protocols that are embedded within the fine-tuning and deployment process of tools like AutoGen. The core methodology involves conducting controlled experiments to measure the homogenization effect of personalized LLMs on scholarly output, then using those empirical results to iteratively engineer specific technical constraints (e.g., prompt diversification requirements, novelty-check mechanisms) and ethical mandates into the guidelines themselves. The ultimate goal is to produce a dynamic, evidence-based policy framework that actively counters homogenization by making the preservation and enhancement of novel thought a measurable and enforceable standard for ethical LLM use in academic writing.

ideas.title: Assume AI can generate novel, valid research hypotheses
ideas.description: Test the capability of LLMs to produce original, testable research questions in bioethics.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Synthetic Data Provenance as a Control Mechanism for AI Bias and Error
ideas.description: This research direction proposes developing a comprehensive provenance and labeling standard for synthetic data that explicitly encodes information about its generative source, intended purpose, and known limitations. This structured metadata is then designed to be ingested and utilized by specialized auditing and control tools, enabling researchers to map how specific biases or artifacts in the training data correlate with emergent errors or unfair behaviors in the final AI model. By making the synthetic data's 'pedigree' machine-actionable, the approach shifts control from passive documentation to active intervention, allowing for targeted model adjustments or data remediation. This creates a closed-loop system where disclosure standards directly empower the understanding and mitigation of complex AI failures that originate in synthetic data pipelines.

ideas.title: Adapting Supply Chain Provenance for AI Manuscript Contributions
ideas.description: The original idea proposes adapting data lineage frameworks to track AI contributions to manuscripts. By drawing an analogy to Supply Chain Management and Logistics, we find a parallel problem: tracking the origin and transformation of components in a complex assembly process. The analogous solution uses unique identifiers and distributed ledger technology for immutable, step-by-step provenance tracking. Adapted to the research context, this involves modeling the manuscript creation process as an intellectual supply chain. Each discrete AI intervention (e.g., GPT-4 for draft generation, Code Interpreter for a figure, a literature search tool) is tagged with a metadata token recording its model, prompt, timestamp, and input data hash. These tokens are linked in a provenance graph, stored in a lightweight ledger (like a Merkle DAG), providing a transparent, verifiable, and citable record of AI contributions. This system moves beyond coarse disclosure statements to offer detailed, machine-readable lineage, addressing challenges of attribution, reproducibility, and ethical transparency in AI-augmented research.

ideas.title: Challenge that human oversight is always sufficient
ideas.description: Explore scenarios where human authors cannot effectively supervise AI-generated content.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of AUTOGEN and Similar Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Integrity of Scholarly Writing

ideas.title: Immunological AI: A Self-Critiquing System for AI-Generated Text
ideas.description: This research adapts the immunological solution of training a system to distinguish 'self' from 'non-self' to the problem of critiquing AI-generated text. In immunology, vaccines introduce modified threats to safely train immune recognition. Analogously, we propose developing an AI critic system trained on a controlled corpus of verified human-authored ('self') and AI-generated/problematic ('non-self') text. This system would learn to produce specialized 'critique vectors' that act like antibodies, identifying and flagging the characteristic patterns, stylistic artifacts, logical flaws, and integrity violations specific to AI-assisted manuscripts, thereby scaling the critical evaluation of AI-generated academic and scientific text.

ideas.title: Adapt clinical trial protocols for AI tool evaluation
ideas.description: Use randomized controlled trial methodologies to rigorously test new AI tools in the publishing workflow.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Embedded, Dynamic Governance: Integrating Real-Time AI Risk Validation into Research Workflows
ideas.description: This project develops and deploys a technical framework for embedded AI governance in research. Instead of standalone guidelines, it creates plug-in modules for common research environments (e.g., Jupyter, electronic lab notebooks, statistical software) that perform real-time checks for compliance with evolving ethical and methodological standards. These modules are supported by a collaborative platform where the research community documents novel AI use cases, associated risks, and mitigation strategies, which in turn automatically updates the validation protocols. This creates a closed-loop system that makes ethical adherence a seamless part of the research process and adapts dynamically to new tools and practices.

ideas.title: Use adversarial debiasing from ML fairness for AI reviews
ideas.description: Apply machine learning fairness algorithms to detect and mitigate bias in AI-assisted editorial decisions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Assume peer review can be replaced, not just augmented
ideas.description: Design and test fully AI-driven peer review systems without human-in-the-loop.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors' Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing.

ideas.title: An Architectural Framework for Dynamic Attribution and Accountability in Human-LLM Co-Authoring Systems
ideas.description: This research designs and prototypes a technical architecture to make the process of AI-assisted text generation forensically auditable, moving beyond abstract liability debates. The system would instrument the co-authoring process to log user inputs, model parameters, and probabilistically attribute output segments to specific training data influences. By generating a verifiable interaction transcript and source provenance report, it creates the necessary evidence base for any subsequent legal or ethical assessment, making accountability a function of system design rather than just legal interpretation.

ideas.title: Develop ethical guidelines for personalized LLM use
ideas.description: Create specific ethical and policy frameworks for using fine-tuned LLMs like AUTOGEN in academic writing.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of AUTOGEN and Similar Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Integrity of Scholarly Writing

ideas.title: Applying Pharmaceutical Liability Frameworks to AI-Generated Text
ideas.description: This research seed investigates legal accountability for errors or plagiarism in text produced by personalized LLMs by drawing an analogy from pharmaceutical regulation. The analogous field is pharmaceutical product liability, which uses a multi-tiered framework to distribute responsibility among the manufacturer, physician, and pharmacist based on their role and control over the product's risk. Adapting this solution, the research will propose and analyze a legal framework that clarifies and apportions responsibility among the LLM developer (for foundational model integrity), the service provider implementing personalization (for modifications and safeguards), and the end-user (for specific instructions and use-case application), thereby establishing a clear chain of accountability for AI-generated content.

ideas.title: Measuring the Impact of Personalized LLMs on Scholarly Divergence: A Controlled Experiment in Idea Generation and Diffusion
ideas.description: This research designs a controlled experiment to test if LLM-assisted scholarly work reduces conceptual divergence. Participants are assigned to conditions with different levels and types of LLM support during a literature review and hypothesis-generation task. Originality is measured through computational metrics of textual novelty against a corpus, blind expert ratings of contribution novelty, and the subsequent network analysis of how ideas from different conditions spread in a simulated peer discussion. The study isolates the effect of LLM personalization on both the production and the reception of scholarly ideas.

ideas.title: Forensic Attribution for Data Provenance and Ethical Auditing in AI Training
ideas.description: This research applies forensic linguistics and stylometry not to the output of AI models, but to their training data. It develops techniques to detect and attribute the human-authored texts within a training corpus, identifying specific authors, publications, or stylistic clusters without explicit prior labeling. This enables a concrete, evidence-based audit of a model's training data to determine if it contains copyrighted material, personal writings, or other content used without proper consent or licensing. The resulting framework provides a technical tool for legal and ethical accountability, transforming the assumption of 'ethically unproblematic' data into a falsifiable claim grounded in linguistic evidence.

ideas.title: Apply version control systems to track human-AI collaboration
ideas.description: Use Git-like systems from software development to document the iterative writing process between author and AI.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Transfer open-source software licensing to AI content
ideas.description: Apply copyleft and attribution models from software to manage AI-generated scholarly material.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors' Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing.

ideas.title: Empirical study of AI's impact on peer review quality
ideas.description: Measure how AI-assisted peer review affects feedback depth, bias, and editorial decisions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors' Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing.

ideas.title: Informed Consent in Medicine as a Model for Layered AI Transparency
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that explaining AI use to non-experts is always sufficient for transparency by drawing an analogy to informed consent in medical ethics. In medicine, simply providing a patient-friendly brochure is insufficient for true consent; instead, a layered, interactive process tailored to the individual's comprehension and curiosity is required. Adapting this solution, the research proposes that AI transparency systems must move beyond one-off non-expert disclosures. They should implement a tiered framework where a clear, core explanation is the entry point, but users are actively guided to accessible deeper layers of technical detail, audit results, and accountability mechanisms, with the system designed to verify and respond to the user's level of understanding, thereby creating a more robust and accountable transparency practice.

ideas.title: Forensic Attribution and Liability for AI-Induced Stylistic Degradation
ideas.description: This research investigates how the forensic detection of stylistic degradation—such as loss of authorial voice, homogenization, or introduced inconsistencies—can serve as evidence in legal proceedings to assign accountability for errors or harms in AI-generated text. It aims to develop methodologies that not only identify AI use but also quantify the specific 'degradation footprint' attributable to a particular AI system or its human operator, thereby clarifying the chain of causation. The core legal question becomes whether liability falls on the user for negligent prompting, the developer for a system that inherently degrades style in misleading ways, or both, based on the forensic analysis of the output. This creates a new, evidence-based bridge between computational linguistics and legal standards of care and causation.

ideas.title: Adapting Cryptographic Zero-Knowledge Proofs for AI Writing Tool Audits
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from cryptography—specifically, zero-knowledge proofs (ZKPs)—to the problem of auditing and validating AI writing tools, analogous to transferring peer review models. In cryptography, ZKPs allow for the verification of a claim's truth without exposing the underlying sensitive data. The adapted research idea applies this principle to create a formal audit protocol for AI writing systems. An AI model (the prover) would generate a proof alongside its text output, demonstrating to an independent auditor (the verifier) that the output complies with predefined standards (e.g., originality thresholds, safety filters, attribution rules) without revealing the model's proprietary training data or full parameters. This method provides a rigorous, automated, and privacy-preserving validation mechanism, transferring the 'blind' trust aspect of peer review into a technical, scalable framework for AI oversight.

ideas.title: Challenge necessity of human authorship for all content
ideas.description: Explore frameworks for crediting AI as a co-author or agent under specific, defined conditions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors' Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing.

ideas.title: Challenge the necessity of human-only peer review
ideas.description: Experiment with a peer review model where AI conducts first-pass analysis before human review.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Metrological Uncertainty Quantification for AI-Generated Text Detection
ideas.description: This research applies the systematic error analysis and uncertainty quantification frameworks from metrology to the algorithms used for detecting AI-generated text. Instead of producing a simple similarity score or binary classification, the approach models and reports the measurement uncertainty of the detection tool itself, accounting for factors like training data bias, model architecture limitations, and input text ambiguity. This yields a detection report with a confidence interval and an uncertainty budget, specifying the major sources of potential error. Consequently, it provides a standardized, auditable, and scientifically robust foundation for using such tools in high-stakes academic, journalistic, and legal contexts where the reliability of evidence is paramount.

ideas.title: Challenge that human oversight is always necessary
ideas.description: Investigate scenarios where fully automated editorial processes are acceptable or superior.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors' Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing.

ideas.title: Continuous Adversarial Multi-Agent Auditing for Dynamic AI Oversight
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves beyond the static, human-paced model of peer review to a continuous, automated auditing paradigm. It employs a dynamic collective of specialized AI agents that act as adversarial reviewers, perpetually stress-testing the target AI system for a comprehensive range of failure modes in a secure environment. A meta-reviewer agent synthesizes these adversarial results into actionable oversight reports and risk scores. The criteria and techniques of the auditing agent collective are themselves periodically reviewed and updated by a human oversight committee, ensuring the system adapts to novel threats while maintaining rigorous, transparent standards.

ideas.title: Scale to different disciplines: STEM vs. humanities AI use
ideas.description: Compare how AI use policies must differ for technical fields versus interpretive humanities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Assume fine-tuning data is ethically unproblematic
ideas.description: Scrutinize copyright and consent issues in using personal publications for model training.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of AUTOGEN and Similar Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Integrity of Scholarly Writing

ideas.title: Detect AI via Stylistic Entropy Loss: A Signal Processing Approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from digital signal processing and audio forensics to the problem of AI text detection. In audio, lossy compression degrades the original signal in measurable ways, such as reducing entropy and introducing statistical artifacts; forensic tools detect this degradation to prove the audio is not the original master. Analogously, we propose treating an author's unique writing style as a complex, high-entropy signal. AI generation, by averaging over vast training data, acts as a 'stylistic compressor,' systematically reducing this entropy and smoothing out unique, high-complexity authorial fingerprints. The adapted research idea is to develop detection methods that measure this loss—quantifying reductions in stylistic entropy, syntactical burstiness, or the distribution of rare lexical choices—to identify text that has passed through an AI 'compression' filter, thereby detecting AI use via the degradation of the authorial signal rather than the presence of foreign artifacts.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: AI to generate undetectable misconduct
ideas.description: Use adversarial AI to generate synthetic misconduct cases for stress-testing and improving detection tools.
ideas.sourceTitle: Guidance needed for using artificial intelligence to screen journal submissions for misconduct

ideas.title: Adversarial Linguistic Game for Proactive AI Authorship Attribution
ideas.description: This research designs a closed-loop, adversarial framework where AI attribution detectors are continuously trained and evaluated against a dynamically updated suite of probe language models. These probe models are engineered to simulate both current and plausible near-future AI writing styles, including those that mimic specific human authorship or evade known forensic markers. The system generates a constantly evolving benchmark dataset, forcing attribution techniques to become more generalized and robust. This proactive, model-aware approach aims to stay ahead of the curve in the AI authorship arms race rather than reacting to it.

ideas.title: Use plagiarism detection algorithms for AI text
ideas.description: Modify existing text-matching algorithms to identify AI-generated content and self-plagiarism from AI outputs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors' Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing.

ideas.title: Perceptual Forensics: Quantifying Reader Trust in AI-Assisted Scholarship Through Detectable Linguistic Signals
ideas.description: This research program integrates forensic stylometric analysis with reader-response studies to investigate the relationship between detectable AI linguistic features and reader trust. It empirically tests how readers' perceptions of credibility, authority, and trustworthiness change when they are presented with scholarship containing forensic markers identified as indicative of AI assistance. The core innovation is using forensic techniques to systematically vary the type and intensity of AI signals in experimental texts, thereby creating a causal map between specific linguistic artifacts and reader judgment. This enables a predictive model of trust erosion or calibration based not on generic disclosure, but on the presence of identifiable stylistic features.

ideas.title: Establish standards for synthetic data disclosure and labeling
ideas.description: Develop clear protocols for indicating, labeling, and describing the generation and use of synthetic data.
ideas.sourceTitle: The ethics of using artificial intelligence in scientific research: new guidance needed for a new tool

ideas.title: Quantifying and Modeling the Stylistic Degradation Signature of AI-Assisted Writing
ideas.description: This research program first conducts longitudinal empirical studies to precisely measure how different levels of AUTOGEN assistance affect specific, quantifiable dimensions of authorial voice and stylistic coherence in human writers. These controlled measurements are then used to define a 'degradation signature'—a multi-dimensional profile of stylistic loss. This signature becomes the foundation for a new inverse detection model that identifies AI use not by looking for AI-like features, but by forensically detecting the absence or erosion of uniquely human stylistic consistency that the empirical studies mapped. The synergy enables a feedback loop where detection models are validated against ground-truth degradation data, and empirical findings are refined by the model's sensitivity to specific stylistic flaws.

ideas.title: The Accountability Scaffold: Measuring Transparency Gaps Between Public Disclosure and Enforceable Oversight
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework to diagnose and address the 'transparency gap' in AI systems, where non-expert disclosure is necessary but insufficient for accountability. It defines three mandatory channels of transparency: public explanation, auditor-accessible documentation, and enforceable rights to contestation. The system then develops metrics to identify when and why a gap exists between public disclosure and the technical/procedural access required for meaningful oversight in specific high-risk contexts. The outcome is a validated scaffold for designing and regulating AI transparency that ensures disclosure translates to actionable accountability.

ideas.title: Apply bias auditing from algorithmic fairness to research AI
ideas.description: Use established algorithmic fairness audit frameworks to systematically assess bias in research AI tools.
ideas.sourceTitle: The ethics of using artificial intelligence in scientific research: new guidance needed for a new tool

ideas.title: Participatory Disclosure: Co-designing AI Transparency Artifacts with Non-Expert Stakeholders
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using participatory design (PD) methods not just for general stakeholder engagement, but specifically to co-create the mechanisms and artifacts of AI disclosure and transparency. It directly challenges the sufficiency of one-way, expert-defined explanations by involving non-expert stakeholders—such as end-users, impacted communities, or frontline workers—in designing what transparency means, what information is needed, and how it should be communicated in their specific context. This process moves beyond assessing if a given explanation is understandable to actively defining the criteria for sufficient transparency through collective sensemaking. The outcome is a framework for generating situated, legitimate transparency protocols that are inherently more accountable because they are built with, not just for, the people they are intended to serve.

ideas.title: Apply provenance tracking from digital forensics
ideas.description: Use cryptographic provenance methods to create immutable audit trails for AI-generated content in manuscripts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors' Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Impact Assessment Frameworks to Measure AUTOGEN's Influence on the Writing Ecosystem
ideas.description: This research adapts methodologies from ecology and environmental science, specifically frameworks for assessing the impact of introduced species or pollutants on an ecosystem. The analogous problem is measuring how a new agent changes a complex, interdependent system. The solution involves establishing pre-intervention baselines, using controlled introductions, and tracking multi-dimensional changes over time. Adapted to AUTOGEN, the study would first define and measure key 'ecological' variables of the writing landscape: quality (productivity/efficiency), originality (novelty/diversity of ideas), and authorial voice (stylistic diversity and authenticity). Researchers would then conduct empirical, longitudinal studies with writer cohorts (e.g., students, professionals, hobbyists) assigned to different levels of AUTOGEN integration (from full use to no use as a control). By systematically comparing the 'ecosystem' outputs—the texts produced—against the baseline and control, the study aims to isolate and quantify AUTOGEN's specific impact on the health and evolution of writing practices, moving beyond anecdotal evidence to a systemic, evidence-based assessment.

ideas.title: Macro-ethics: AI's impact on global research ecosystems
ideas.description: Investigate how widespread AI adoption reshapes global research collaboration, competition, and equity.
ideas.sourceTitle: The ethics of using artificial intelligence in scientific research: new guidance needed for a new tool

ideas.title: AI as primary arbiter with human appeal
ideas.description: Invert the human-verification model, making AI the initial decision-maker subject to author appeal.
ideas.sourceTitle: Guidance needed for using artificial intelligence to screen journal submissions for misconduct

ideas.title: Forensic Attribution of Institutional AI Models via Stylometric Fingerprinting
ideas.description: This research direction develops forensic linguistic techniques specifically tailored to attribute generated text not just to a generic AI, but to a specific fine-tuned institutional or departmental LLM. By analyzing a lab's unique corpus and the resulting model's stylistic 'fingerprint', it becomes possible to detect if model outputs have been used externally, identify intellectual property leakage, or audit compliance with internal usage policies. This transforms institutional LLMs from black-box tools into accountable systems whose textual outputs can be forensically traced back to their source, merging scalable model customization with verifiable authorship attribution.

ideas.title: Scale down: AI micro-ethics for single-sentence generation
ideas.description: Study ethical norms for using AI to phrase a single sentence versus entire sections.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Applying Antibiotic Resistance Monitoring to LLM-Induced Scholarly Homogenization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that personalized LLMs enhance scholarly work by positing they may homogenize thought and reduce novel contributions. Drawing an analogy from microbiology's approach to antibiotic resistance—where overuse of broad-spectrum drugs reduces microbial diversity and long-term efficacy—we adapt its surveillance and stewardship solution. The adapted research involves creating a 'Thought Diversity Surveillance' system to empirically measure homogenization in LLM-assisted scholarship, paired with experimental tests of 'LLM stewardship' protocols (e.g., structured prompting for divergence, mandatory model rotation, or ideation phases without LLMs) designed to counteract convergence and preserve originality, analogous to preserving a healthy, diverse intellectual ecosystem.

ideas.title: Adapting Participatory Action Research from Community Psychology for AI Stakeholder Engagement
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Participatory Action Research (PAR) methodology from the field of Community Psychology to structure AI research stakeholder engagement. The analogous solution, PAR, involves researchers and community members in a cyclical process of co-defining problems, collaboratively designing and implementing interventions, and collectively evaluating outcomes to ensure research is relevant and empowering. Adapted to the AI research context, this means moving beyond one-off participatory design workshops to establish long-term, iterative cycles where stakeholders (impacted communities, domain experts, policymakers, etc.) are true partners. They would co-define the AI research problem, co-design prototypes, audits, or governance frameworks, participate in their implementation, and jointly assess the results. This approach transforms stakeholder engagement from a consultative step into a process of shared ownership and action, ensuring AI research agendas and outputs are directly responsive to stakeholder-identified needs, values, and ethical imperatives.

ideas.title: Transfer consensus conference methods for policy development
ideas.description: Use formal consensus-building techniques from health policy to develop standards for AI use in publishing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing

ideas.title: Apply cybersecurity threat intelligence sharing to misconduct
ideas.description: Adapt Information Sharing and Analysis Centers (ISACs) model for journals to share data on novel misconduct patterns.
ideas.sourceTitle: Guidance needed for using artificial intelligence to screen journal submissions for misconduct

ideas.title: Apply Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) framework to research AI bias auditing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Environmental Science and Regulatory Policy, where Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) provide a structured, phased framework for evaluating potential harms. The analogous EIA solution involves standardized screening, scoping, impact prediction, mitigation, and monitoring with mandatory disclosure. Adapted to the research AI context, this creates a 'Research AI Bias Impact Assessment' framework. This mandated audit would require developers to systematically screen tools for high-risk bias, scope relevant fairness dimensions, predict impacts using algorithmic fairness metrics, create a bias mitigation plan, and establish a monitoring protocol before deployment. The process would require documentation and review by an institutional or funding-body ethics board, introducing a regulatory-style checkpoint to systematically assess and mitigate bias in research AI tools, similar to how EIAs govern projects with potential environmental consequences.

ideas.title: Assume LLMs degrade, not enhance, scholarly originality
ideas.description: Test if personalized LLMs homogenize thought and reduce novel scholarly contributions.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of AUTOGEN and Similar Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Integrity of Scholarly Writing

ideas.title: Dynamic Bias Propagation Tracking for Iterative Research AI
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond one-time bias audits to continuously monitor how biases emerge and compound within an AI-assisted research process. It embeds diagnostic modules that track the evolution of claims, evidence selection, and reasoning chains across a multi-step workflow, identifying points where initial skews become amplified. The system provides real-time feedback to researchers about potential bias propagation and can suggest corrective actions, such as alternative search strategies or challenge questions. This results in a transparent, bias-aware research trajectory rather than just a flagged final output.

ideas.title: Develop frameworks for human-AI review workflows
ideas.description: Design and test specific procedural models for integrating human verification with AI-generated misconduct flags.
ideas.sourceTitle: Guidance needed for using artificial intelligence to screen journal submissions for misconduct

ideas.title: Apply Phylogenetic Signal Analysis to AI Authorship Attribution
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from evolutionary biology and phylogenetics to the problem of AI authorship attribution. In phylogenetics, conserved genetic markers and statistical models (e.g., maximum likelihood) are used to trace evolutionary relationships and attribute unknown samples to specific lineages based on inherited traits—a process analogous to tracing the origin of a text. The adapted idea treats AI text generation models as evolving 'lineages'. It proposes developing a framework to identify conserved 'linguistic markers' (syntactic preferences, semantic frames, token distributions) that act as phylogenetic signals inherited from base models or training data. Statistical phylogenetic models would then be applied to trace the 'ancestry' of a given text, enabling attribution to a specific AI model family (GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA, etc.) or definitive distinction from human authorship by detecting the characteristic phylogenetic signal of machine-generated language.
