ideas.title: Scale-down: AI for niche misconduct in specialized fields
ideas.description: Create tailored AI detectors for field-specific misconduct like phylogenetic tree manipulation or NMR data fabrication.

ideas.title: Metrological Error Analysis for AUTOGEN's Cross-Disciplinary Generalizability
ideas.description: This research fuses the cross-disciplinary testing of AUTOGEN with the rigorous error analysis frameworks of metrology. The core activity is to apply AUTOGEN to diverse academic fields (e.g., law, sociology, engineering) while instrumenting the process with metrological concepts like systematic vs. random error, uncertainty budgets, and traceability. This allows researchers to move beyond simple performance metrics to create detailed error profiles for AUTOGEN in each discipline, identifying whether failures are due to domain-specific knowledge gaps, reasoning biases, or tool limitations. The synergy produces a standardized, quantitative diagnostic for evaluating the transferability and robustness of AI research tools across epistemic boundaries.

ideas.sourceTitle: Editors' Statement on the Responsible Use of Generative AI Technologies in Scholarly Journal Publishing.

ideas.title: Apply Forensic Ballistics Marking Analysis to AI Text Attribution
ideas.description: This research adapts the forensic ballistics principle of toolmark analysis to the problem of AI text detection. In ballistics, experts match fired ammunition to a specific firearm by analyzing unique, microscopic imperfections imparted during manufacturing and use. Analogously, this project proposes that different AI models and training datasets impart unique, subtle 'linguistic toolmarks' on their outputs—patterns in syntax, token probability distributions, phrase constructions, or other low-level features that are consistent for a given source but vary between models. The adapted solution involves developing algorithms to extract and profile these model-specific signatures from known AI-generated text samples, building a database of 'AI ballistic fingerprints.' The goal is to move beyond generic AI detection to precise source attribution, potentially identifying which specific model or training data fork produced a given text, thereby creating a forensic-grade tool for AI text provenance.

ideas.title: Full transparency about AI tools hinders detection
ideas.description: Explore if disclosing specific AI tools and methods enables adversaries to circumvent them more easily.

ideas.title: Empirical studies on AUTOGEN's impact on writing
ideas.description: Conduct studies to measure how AUTOGEN affects writing quality, originality, and authorial voice in practice.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of AUTOGEN and Similar Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Integrity of Scholarly Writing

ideas.title: Proactive Structural Frameworks for Bias-Safe AI Development
ideas.description: This research develops and validates mandatory procedural frameworks that engineer bias prevention directly into the AI development lifecycle. Instead of just tools for finding bias, it provides concrete specifications for equitable data sourcing, inclusive design teams, algorithmic transparency logs, and continuous impact assessments that are required before deployment. The goal is to create auditable development pathways that make it structurally difficult to produce widely biased systems, moving the field from describing harm to preventing its creation.

ideas.title: Micro-Task AI via Modular Enzymatic Architecture
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from synthetic biology, where complex processes are decomposed into specialized enzymatic modules, to the domain of AI assistance. The analogous problem of achieving high specificity and efficiency in multi-step biochemical synthesis is solved using discrete, optimized enzymes for each micro-step. The adapted idea is to develop LLMs not as general assistants, but as a library of ultra-specialized 'AI enzymatic' modules, each dedicated to a single, atomic micro-task like citation formatting or grammar rule application. These lightweight, task-specific modules would be orchestrated by a simple sequencer to perform complex editing workflows, thereby scaling down model size and up task precision, mirroring the efficiency and specificity of a modular metabolic pathway.

ideas.title: Use cryptographic watermarking for AI-generated text
ideas.description: Adapt media watermarking techniques to indelibly tag AI-produced content in submissions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editors’ statement on the responsible use of generative AI technologies in scholarly journal publishing

ideas.title: A Power-Structure Diagnostic for Differentiating Feasible from Co-opted Stakeholder Engagement in AI Research
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a diagnostic framework to assess the preconditions for meaningful stakeholder engagement in AI-driven projects across diverse global contexts. It moves beyond a one-size-fits-all prescription by first analyzing the political economy, governance structures, and power asymmetries that enable or subvert participatory processes. The output is a typology of engagement environments, guiding practitioners on when and how to pursue direct engagement, when to seek alternative accountability mechanisms, and when to avoid engagement that risks legitimizing harm. The goal is to replace blanket advocacy with context-sensitive, power-aware strategies for responsible AI development.

ideas.title: Adapting Air Traffic Control's System-Wide Monitoring to AI Content Supervision
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that human oversight is always sufficient for supervising AI-generated content by drawing an analogy from Air Traffic Control. In ATC, human controllers cannot directly see or mentally track every aircraft; instead, they rely on system-wide monitoring tools like TCAS and automated conflict alerts that provide synthesized situational awareness and critical alerts. Adapting this solution, the research proposes to develop analogous system-level monitoring tools for AI content ecosystems. These tools would shift the human supervisor's role from reviewing individual content items—a task that becomes impossible at scale and complexity—to interpreting meta-patterns, anomalous generation signatures, and systemic risk alerts, thereby managing the health of the information environment at a strategic level rather than through direct, granular oversight.

ideas.title: Stakeholder Sovereignty Systems: Enforceable Co-Governance Protocols for AI Research
ideas.description: This research designs and tests enforceable institutional systems that grant impacted communities direct authority over AI research affecting them. It moves beyond advisory engagement to establish binding agreements on shared control, including community veto powers for high-risk applications, jointly managed funds for independent impact assessment, and mandatory integration of stakeholder-defined requirements into technical specifications. The outcome is a verifiable governance framework that ensures community concerns directly shape and, where necessary, constrain the AI research and development lifecycle.

ideas.title: Orchestrated Micro-Task Composition for Cohesive AI Assistance
ideas.description: This research develops a unified AI assistant framework where a lightweight central orchestrator manages a library of specialized, fine-tuned micro-task modules (e.g., for citation formatting, grammar checking, jargon simplification). The orchestrator interprets the user's overarching goal, breaks it into a sequence of micro-tasks, executes the appropriate modules in order while preserving context, and returns a cohesive result. This approach maintains the efficiency and precision of task-specific models while providing a seamless, integrated user experience that can handle complex, multi-step objectives.

ideas.title: Graph-Based Pedigree Tracking for Complex Synthetic Data Lineages
ideas.description: This research develops a provenance system for synthetic data inspired by the pedigree documentation of advanced materials, but designed for digital, non-linear creation processes. It models the entire synthetic data generation pipeline as a provenance graph, capturing all precursor datasets, model versions, code, parameters, and their intricate relationships. The framework uses immutable logging and standard metadata formats to create a auditable record for every generated data point. This allows users to trace synthetic data back to its origins, understand the influence of each component, and rigorously assess biases, copyright implications, and reproducibility.

ideas.title: Test AUTOGEN across diverse academic disciplines

ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of AUTOGEN and Similar Fine-Tuned Large Language Models on the Integrity of Scholarly Writing

ideas.title: Adapting Benchmarking Protocols from Machine Learning to Test AUTOGEN's Cross-Disciplinary Generalizability
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of testing AUTOGEN across disciplines by drawing an analogy from Machine Learning, where the problem of assessing a model's general capabilities was solved through the creation of comprehensive, multi-task benchmark suites. The analogous solution involved curating diverse, standardized tasks to produce a systematic performance profile. Adapting this, the research would develop 'DISCIPLINE-BENCH,' a curated suite of argument-generation or analytical tasks sourced from diverse academic fields such as law, climate science, economics, and history. Each task, designed with domain experts, would probe different reasoning facets relevant to each discipline. By applying AUTOGEN to this benchmark and conducting a rigorous, quantitative analysis of its performance variations, the study would move beyond anecdotal testing to produce a detailed, evidence-based assessment of AUTOGEN's cross-disciplinary generalizability, limitations, and adaptation requirements.

ideas.title: Apply financial audit trails to AI use disclosure

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Trial Protocols for Systematic AI Bias Auditing
ideas.description: This research adapts the rigorous, phased protocol structure from clinical drug trials to create a practical framework for AI bias identification and control. The analogous problem in clinical pharmacology—systematically identifying adverse and biased effects of a new treatment across diverse human populations—was solved through standardized trial phases with strict controls, blinding, and pre-registered analysis plans. The adapted idea is to develop a formal 'AI Clinical Trial' protocol. This framework would provide researchers and auditors with a mandatory, step-by-step methodology to expose an AI system to stratified test cohorts (representing different demographics, edge cases, and environments) in a controlled setting (Phase I: lab, Phase II/III: limited deployment, Phase IV: post-market surveillance). By incorporating blinding, control groups, and pre-specified statistical measures of fairness and error, this protocol offers a practical, standardized method to objectively identify, describe, and quantify biases, creating a clear evidence trail for decisions about model mitigation, control, and deployment readiness.

ideas.title: Relax assumption that disclosure ensures responsibility

ideas.title: Purpose-Bound Provenance: A Framework for Governing Synthetic Data Identity Beyond Demarcation
ideas.description: This research proposes a paradigm shift from trying to detect synthetic data to formally attesting and governing its origin and permissible uses. The system designs protocols to embed cryptographically verifiable provenance information (watermarks, lineage graphs) at the moment of synthetic data generation, creating a persistent, tamper-evident link to its synthetic source and generation parameters. This attested provenance then enables purpose-bound control mechanisms, where data use is constrained by predefined ethical, legal, or operational intents (e.g., training only, audit only). The framework thus ensures synthetic data remains functionally useful while being accountable and governable, turning its synthetic identity into a cornerstone of trust rather than a problem of detection.

ideas.title: Discipline-Adaptive Framework for Testing AUTOGEN's Epistemic Generalizability
ideas.description: This research develops and applies a structured framework to test the AUTOGEN approach's adaptability to fundamentally different academic epistemologies. It begins with a comparative analysis of target disciplines (e.g., theoretical physics, clinical psychology, constitutional law) to explicitly characterize their distinct knowledge-validation practices. The framework then guides the customization of AUTOGEN simulations—including agent roles, interaction protocols, and success criteria—to align with each discipline's epistemic norms. This ensures the assessment measures meaningful generalizability across deep methodological divides, not just superficial applicability.

ideas.title: Institutional LLM Fine-Tuning via Federated Model Aggregation
ideas.description: This idea proposes scaling fine-tuned LLMs to institutional levels by drawing an analogy from computational ecology and population genetics. In landscape genetics, models aggregate local genetic variation from isolated subpopulations to understand broader adaptation. Similarly, for institutional LLM fine-tuning, the solution is to avoid centralizing a lab or department's entire sensitive corpus. Instead, the adapted method involves fine-tuning separate LLM instances or adapters on local, isolated data subsets (e.g., from individual research groups within the department). An aggregation algorithm then synthesizes the learned parameter adjustments or adapter modules from these local models to create a unified, institutional-scale model. This federated approach captures the shared linguistic patterns, jargon, and knowledge domain of the institution while preserving data privacy and security by preventing raw data from ever leaving its local silo.

ideas.title: Guaranteeing AI Safety via Behavioral Constraints and Monitoring Under Partial Observability
ideas.description: This research program accepts that full mechanistic understanding of advanced AI may be unattainable and shifts the focus to ensuring safety through external verification. It develops frameworks for defining and enforcing behavioral guarantees—such as fairness bounds or output robustness—that must hold regardless of the system's internal state. The approach combines runtime monitoring, adversarial probing, and formal methods applied to the AI's observable behavior to detect and correct deviations, creating a controllable 'envelope' of operation without requiring complete transparency.

ideas.title: Ontological Bridge for Causal Error Tracing from AI Systems to Metrological Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops a formal ontology to map the emergent, systemic failures of AI tools (e.g., discriminatory bias, robustness failures) onto a causal-probabilistic graph of traceable technical processes and data attributes. This bridge enables the application of rigorous metrological concepts, such as error decomposition and uncertainty propagation, to AI evaluation. The resulting framework produces not just a performance score, but a structured, auditable uncertainty budget that attributes portions of the total observed error to specific, addressable causes in the data, model, and deployment context.

ideas.title: Applying Immunological 'Tolerance' Concepts to Detect and Control AI Bias
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that researchers can fully understand and control complex AI biases by drawing an analogy from immunology, specifically the biological concept of immune tolerance. The analogous problem—how the body distinguishes its own tissues from foreign pathogens to prevent autoimmune disease—was solved through multi-layered mechanisms of central and peripheral tolerance. We adapt this solution by proposing a novel AI governance framework where 'tolerance' to desired, unbiased operation is engineered. The adapted idea involves creating AI systems with two key components: a 'central tolerance' module integrated during training to eliminate fundamental bias-prone representations, and a 'peripheral tolerance' subsystem that operates during deployment, using specialized watchdog models to detect, isolate, and correct biased outputs in real-time, thereby offering a bio-inspired, layered approach to control.

ideas.title: Applying Cryptographic Watermarking and Provenance Chains to Demarcate Synthetic Data
ideas.description: This idea explores a technical solution to the blurring boundary between synthetic and real data by drawing an analogy from digital forensics and cryptography. In those fields, the authenticity and lineage of digital artifacts are secured using cryptographic hashing, watermarking, and immutable provenance chains. Adapting this solution, the research would investigate frameworks for embedding cryptographically verifiable markers of origin—a form of 'synthetic data watermark' or provenance record—at the point of data generation. This shifts the core problem from post-hoc statistical detection to the technical verification of a declared and secure provenance, creating a clear, contestable, and auditable demarcation line based on lineage rather than content similarity.

ideas.title: Adapt archaeological stratigraphy and context recording for synthetic data lineage
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from archaeology, where meticulous stratigraphy and context recording (like the Harris Matrix) preserve the provenance and relationships of artifacts within an immutable layered record. The analogous solution documents the precise position and interdependencies of findings, allowing reconstruction of the discovery process even if the original site is gone. Adapted to synthetic data, this means creating a stratigraphic provenance system where each generative step (model version, prompt, parameter set, random seed) is recorded as a discrete 'context' with defined relationships to prior and subsequent steps. This forms a lineage graph akin to an archaeological section drawing, providing an auditable, reconstructable chain-of-custody that captures not just the data's origin but the full layered context of its creation, enabling verification, reproducibility, and trust assessment long after the original generative pipelines have evolved or been retired.

ideas.title: Federated Modular Adapters for Dynamic Institutional Knowledge Synthesis
ideas.description: This system replaces monolithic lab-specific models with a federated architecture centered on a shared institutional base model. Individual labs or teams privately train small, specialized adapter modules on their own corpora. An intelligent query router analyzes incoming requests and dynamically composes relevant adapters—potentially from multiple labs—with the base model to generate responses. This design breaks down knowledge silos by enabling cross-disciplinary synthesis while rigorously preserving data privacy and autonomy for each group.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Fate and Transport Modeling for AI Error Propagation
ideas.description: This idea adapts systematic error analysis frameworks from metrology to AI by drawing a direct analogy from environmental science. In ecotoxicology, scientists use fate and transport models to predict how pollutants spread and transform through interconnected environmental compartments (e.g., air to water to soil to organisms), quantifying how source uncertainties propagate to final exposure risks. The analogous solution is to model an AI research tool as a series of interconnected compartments (data ingestion, preprocessing, model training, validation, inference). The adapted research idea applies this compartmental modeling approach to trace the propagation of initial errors—such as data measurement noise, labeling inaccuracies, or algorithmic approximations—through the AI pipeline. By modeling transformation and accumulation processes at each stage (similar to degradation, adsorption, or bioaccumulation), we can build a causal map of uncertainty sources and systematically quantify their contribution to the final output's total error, providing a more holistic and interpretable uncertainty quantification framework for AI systems.
